references.title: Regulation (EU) No 575/2013 — Capital Requirements Regulation (CRR)
references.description: The core prudential rulebook for EU banks: own funds, credit risk (standardised and IRB), large exposures, liquidity and reporting. Use the consolidated version on EUR-Lex for the current text.

references.title: Regulation (EU) 2024/1623 — CRR3 (Basel III implementation)
references.description: The amending regulation implementing final Basel III in the EU: output floor, revised standardised approach, IRB constraints (removal of A-IRB for large corporates, input floors), applicable from 1 January 2025.

references.title: Directive 2013/36/EU — Capital Requirements Directive (CRD IV)
references.description: The directive half of the framework: authorisation, supervisory review (SREP), buffers, governance and remuneration.

references.title: Directive (EU) 2024/1619 — CRD VI
references.description: Companion directive to CRR3: supervisory powers, third-country branches, ESG risk management requirements and fit-and-proper rules.

references.title: European Commission — Banking Package (CRR3/CRD VI) overview
references.description: The Commission's landing page for the 2021 banking package with legislative history, impact assessments and delegated acts.

references.title: EBA Interactive Single Rulebook
references.description: Article-by-article view of CRR/CRD with all associated technical standards, guidelines and Q&As attached. The fastest way from an article number to everything that hangs off it.

references.title: EBA Single Rulebook Q&A tool
references.description: Searchable database of official interpretations of CRR/CRD questions. Practically binding for supervisory dialogue.

references.title: EBA Roadmap on the Banking Package (Basel III implementation)
references.description: The EBA's plan and timeline for the ~140 mandates under CRR3/CRD VI — the master schedule for what technical standards land when.

references.title: EBA Guidelines on PD estimation, LGD estimation and defaulted assets (EBA/GL/2017/16)
references.description: The core IRB modelling standard: how to build and calibrate PD and LGD models, margin of conservatism, treatment of defaulted assets.

references.title: EBA Guidelines on the definition of default (EBA/GL/2016/07)
references.description: Harmonised application of the Article 178 default definition: days past due, unlikeliness to pay, materiality thresholds, return to non-default.

references.title: EBA Guidelines on downturn LGD estimation (EBA/GL/2019/03)
references.description: How to identify downturn periods and quantify downturn adjustments in LGD estimates — completes the IRB repair programme with GL/2017/16.

references.title: EBA Guidelines on credit risk mitigation for A-IRB institutions (EBA/GL/2020/05)
references.description: Eligibility and recognition of guarantees and collateral when using own LGD estimates.

references.title: EBA Guidelines on management of non-performing and forborne exposures (EBA/GL/2018/06)
references.description: NPE strategy, governance and operational requirements for banks with elevated NPL levels; the framework behind NPE reporting and forbearance.

references.title: EBA Guidelines on loan origination and monitoring (EBA/GL/2020/06)
references.description: End-to-end credit-granting standards: creditworthiness assessment, collateral valuation, pricing frameworks and ongoing monitoring.

references.title: ECB Guide to internal models (2024 revision)
references.description: The ECB's supervisory expectations for IRB (and other) models: model governance, data, validation, return-to-standardised. What model teams are inspected against.

references.title: ECB TRIM project report (targeted review of internal models)
references.description: Findings from the ECB's multi-year on-site review of internal models — still the reference for common IRB findings and supervisory follow-up.

references.title: ECB Guidance to banks on non-performing loans
references.description: Supervisory expectations for NPL strategy, governance, provisioning and collateral valuation, extended by the 2018 addendum on provisioning backstops.

references.title: BCBS — The Basel Framework (consolidated)
references.description: The consolidated global standards; the CRE chapters cover credit risk (standardised, IRB, CRM, securitisation) that CRR3 transposes.

references.title: Basel III: Finalising post-crisis reforms (December 2017)
references.description: The 'Basel IV' package: revised standardised approach, IRB constraints and the 72.5% output floor — the source text behind CRR3.

references.title: PRA PS9/24 — Implementation of Basel 3.1: near-final rules part 2 (credit risk)
references.description: The UK's near-final credit risk rules under Basel 3.1 — the comparison point for EU/UK divergence on standardised and IRB treatment.

items.title: EBF calls for quick solutions to close Europe’s Investment Gap
items.summary:
PRESS RELEASE
EBF calls for quick solutions to close Europe’s Investment Gap 
In response to the publication of the EC report on bank competitiveness
Brussels, 17 July 2026 – The European Banking Federation (EBF) welcomes the European Commission’s bank competitiveness report and calls on EU policymakers for an ambitious banking package, emphasising the need for speed. The Commission has identified the right challenges and proposes a clear direction for the way forward. The next step is to translate this momentum into concrete actions that help unlock investment and strengthen Europe’s competit

items.title: The EBA publishes its final draft technical standards on material acquisitions, material transfers, mergers and divisions under the Capital Requirements Directive
items.summary:
The EBA publishes its final draft technical standards on material acquisitions, material transfers, mergers and divisions under the Capital Requirements Directive
The European Banking Authority (EBA) today published its final draft Regulatory Technical Standards (RTS) and Implementing Technical Standards (ITSs) on material acquisitions, transfers of assets or liabilities, mergers and divisions involving credit institutions or (mixed) financial holding companies under the Capital Requirements Directive (CRD). The standards aim to support banking consolidation and deepen EU market integration by

items.title: The EBA consults on amendments to data collection for the 2027 market risk benchmarking exercise
items.summary:
The EBA consults on amendments to data collection for the 2027 market risk benchmarking exercise
The European Banking Authority (EBA) today launched a consultation on amendments to the Implementing Technical Standards (ITS) governing the benchmarking of internal models and the standardised approach for market risk for the 2027 exercise. The proposed amendments aim to ensure that the benchmarking framework remains aligned with the evolving regulatory framework, while providing institutions and supervisors with adequate time to prepare for the implementation of the upcoming market risk requireme

items.title: AFME Welcomes Commission Report on Banking Competitiveness and Calls for Ambitious Reforms
items.summary: The Association for Financial Markets in Europe (AFME) welcomes the European Commission's report on the competitiveness of the EU banking sector, which recognises that a stronger, more integrated banking market is essential to financing Europe's economic growth, strategic autonomy, and green and digital transitions. Caroline Liesegang, Managing Director and Head of Capital & Risk Management at AFME, said: “Europe cannot achieve its competitiveness ambitions without a banking sector that can deploy capital efficiently across borders and support businesses at scale. The Commission's report right

items.title: Building Markets, Creating Opportunity
items.summary: ISDA Chief Executive Officer Scott O'Malia offers informal comments on important OTC derivatives issues in derivatiViews, reflecting ISDA's long-held commitment to making the market safer and more efficient. Deep and liquid derivatives markets are fundamental to the development of well-functioning financial markets and healthy economies. They support lending, investment and financial stability, creating the certainty needed for economic growth.

items.title: Bank loan credit risk pooling: risk diversification versus the moral hazard problem
items.summary: This paper offers a credit risk pooling arrangement which negates the moral hazard problem and improves the risk-return profiles of participating banks.

items.title: CP11/26 – A tailored regime for captive insurance
items.summary:
Privacy statement
By responding to this consultation, you provide personal data to the Bank of England (the Bank, which includes the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA)). This may include your name, contact details (including, if provided, details of the organisation you work for) and opinions or details offered in the response itself. The response will be assessed to inform our work as a regulator and central bank, both in the public interest and in the exercise of our official authority.

items.title: PS16/26 – PRA rule changes to accommodate HM Treasury’s Overseas Prudential Requirements Regime
ECB selects 36 payment service providers to join digital euro pilot
items.summary:
- PRESS RELEASE
ECB selects 36 payment service providers to join digital euro pilot
14 July 2026
- Pilot to take place at the ECB and several Eurosystem national central banks to test digital euro functionalities
- Over 50 applications received, reflecting strong market interest and engagement
- Selected participants span a broad range of business models and sizes and offer broad geographical coverage
The European Central Bank (ECB) has selected 36 payment service providers (PSP) from across the euro area to participate in the digital euro pilot. The pilot exercise is crucial for testing the d

items.title: Regulatory Blog: EBA Simplification Package: Benchmarking und sonstige Reporting-Themen
items.summary:
EBA Simplification Package: Benchmarking und sonstige Reporting-Themen
Modul 9: EBA/CP/2026/07 und EBA/CP/2026/08
Nachdem der zuletzt veröffentlichte Beitrag dieser Blogreihe die geplanten Änderungen am Marktrisiko-Reporting im Fokus hatte, wird die Reihe durch diesen Beitrag abgeschlossen. Schwerpunkt sind einerseits die Integration des aufsichtlichen Benchmarkings in den ITS on Reporting sowie weitere, kleinere Änderungen. Formal sind die weiteren Änderungen im eigenständigen Modul 9 des EBA Konsultationspapiers CP/2026/07 enthalten, während die Vorschläge zum aufsichtlichen Benchmarking ein

items.title: US regulators throw banks a curveball on committed credit lines
items.summary: New Basel III charge on undrawn facilities will hit largest banks in areas like fund finance and credit cards

items.title: EBF Position Paper: Revised EBA Guidelines on limits on exposures to Shadow Banking Entities (SBE)
items.summary: The EBF responded to an EBA consultation on revised guidelines for limits on exposures to Shadow Banking Entities (SBE), advocating to retain the 0.25% materiality threshold, preserve the fallback approach, avoid binding Pillar 1 limits, and establish an official reference list for SBE identification based on Basel Core Principles.
items.why_it_matters: Practitioners need to track this industry feedback as the EBA guidance evolves, since SBE exposure limits directly affect banks' large exposure and counterparty credit risk classification and reporting obligations.

quant.verdict: This is an industry advocacy position on prudential reporting and large exposure rules, but it has no direct bearing on my PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or downturn estimation work.

regulatory.verdict: I need to monitor the EBA's final guidance on SBE exposure limits closely, as the outcome directly shapes how we classify and report counterparty credit risk for our Nordic corporate exposures and affects our large exposure framework.

items.title: ​The EBA releases the final technical package for its 4.3 reporting framework to support Third-Country Branches reporting and AML Authority risk assessment data collection
items.summary: The EBA published the final technical package for reporting framework version 4.3, implementing new supervisory reporting requirements for Third-Country Branches (TCBs) under the Capital Requirements Directive (CRD), effective 31 March 2027. The package includes implementing technical standards, validation rules, data point models, and XBRL taxonomies to support TCB reporting obligations. It also supports the Anti-Money Laundering Authority (AMLA) risk assessment data collection with a reference date of 31 December 2026.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners managing third-country branch operations must prepare for new CRD supervisory reporting obligations with a first reference date of 31 March 2027 and will need to implement the EBA's final technical specifications and validation rules.

quant.verdict: While this is important for compliance and operational reporting, it has limited direct impact on PD/LGD model development, calibration, or validation—the core of my work on IRB credit risk models.

regulatory.verdict: I need to monitor this for any third-country branch exposures we hold, but as a corporate loan book manager at a Nordic bank, this is primarily operational reporting rather than capital treatment relevant—unless our TCB operations themselves hold material corporate credit risk that triggers new reporting classifications.

items.title: Key Trends in the Size and Composition of OTC Derivatives Markets in the Second Half of 2025
items.summary: The article reports BIS data showing OTC derivatives notional outstanding rose 20.7% in H2 2025 versus prior year across all major asset classes. Gross market value increased 29.5% and gross credit exposure rose 13.2%, while close-out netting reduced total exposure by 85.3%. Initial margin posted for cleared derivatives reached $423.5 billion and non-cleared derivative collateral collected reached $1.6 trillion, up 9.3% year-over-year.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to understand the scale and trend of counterparty credit exposure in OTC derivatives markets and the effectiveness of collateralisation and netting frameworks in mitigating it.

quant.verdict: While market-level OTC derivatives trends are useful context for enterprise risk, this macro-level snapshot has no direct bearing on my PD/LGD model development, calibration, data inputs, or validation work—it does not inform corporate default probability estimation or loss-given-default under downturn conditions.

regulatory.verdict: While OTC derivatives market trends are relevant to counterparty credit risk management, this high-level BIS market composition article has no direct bearing on my corporate loan book capital treatment, CRR3 implementation, or supervisory expectations—and our bank's exposure to OTC derivatives is managed separately from corporate credit risk.

items.title: ISDA responds to RBI consultation on SA-CCR
items.summary: ISDA responded to the Reserve Bank of India's consultation on draft directions implementing the standardized approach for counterparty credit risk (SA-CCR), broadly supporting the move while raising five areas for clarification: prior RBI approval requirements for non-zero λj values, the appropriateness of the 1.4 alpha factor, explicit treatment of multi-level client structures, independent legal opinion requirements for qualifying CCP exposures, and porting precedent criteria for assessing CCP portability. ISDA also advocates globally for SA-CCR recognition of cross-product netting between derivatives and securities financing transactions.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners applying SA-CCR under RBI rules need to track the final treatment of approval requirements, alpha factor calibration, and CCP risk weight criteria, as these clarifications will shape capital calculations and supervisory expectations for counterparty exposures.

quant.verdict: This is important for credit risk capital optimization under SA-CCR but not material to my PD/LGD model work—it concerns counterparty credit risk calculation methodology, CCP treatment, and India-specific regulatory clarifications that sit outside corporate exposure modelling and downturn estimation requirements.

regulatory.verdict: This RBI consultation on SA-CCR for Indian counterparty credit risk is outside my purview—I focus on Nordic corporate loan exposures, not Indian derivatives counterparty risk or cross-border CCP treatment under RBI oversight.

items.title: ​The EBA publishes final Guidelines on the authorisation of third-country branches under the Capital Requirements Directive
items.summary: The EBA published final Guidelines on authorisation of third-country branches (TCBs) under CRD6, setting out application requirements, assessment criteria, templates and supervisory process. These Guidelines mandate information disclosure, competent authority assessment standards, and non-opposition statements from third-country home supervisors. The Guidelines form part of the comprehensive TCB regime introduced by CRD6, complementing earlier EBA deliverables on booking arrangements, capital endowment, and reporting requirements.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must understand the new TCB authorisation framework and prudential expectations (capital endowment, booking arrangements, liquidity, governance) that will apply to third-country branch credit risk assessment and supervision.

quant.verdict: I need to monitor these guidelines for any implications to capital requirements and booking location rules for third-country branches in my portfolio, but they do not directly affect PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or downturn estimation.

regulatory.verdict: I monitor this for completeness on CRD6 implementation, but third-country branch authorisation does not affect my corporate credit risk capital treatment or the Nordic corporate loan book directly.

items.title: SA-CCR Own Goal Must be Corrected
items.summary: ISDA Chief Executive comments on calibration issues in the US standardized approach for counterparty credit risk (SA-CCR) under Basel III endgame, arguing that the current methodology leads to disproportionately high capital requirements for cross-margined repo and derivatives portfolios. ISDA recommends incorporating a hedge coverage ratio into SA-CCR to better reflect economic risk and support the planned US Treasury clearing regime beginning end-2026. The article emphasizes that overly conservative capital treatment could constrain bank liquidity provision and hinder the transition to mandatory clearing.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must track SA-CCR calibration and netting rules carefully, as the final US implementation will directly affect capital calculations for derivatives and repo portfolios and supervisory expectations around clearing participation.

quant.verdict: While SA-CCR calibration is important for capital management, this is a capital adequacy issue outside the scope of PD/LGD model development for corporates under IRB; it affects counterparty credit risk under the standardized approach, not my core modeling work.

regulatory.verdict: I monitor SA-CCR calibration closely as it affects our derivatives and repo book capital treatment, but this ISDA commentary on US Basel III endgame implementation carries limited immediate relevance to our Nordic corporate loan book—I'll track the final rule for any spillover effects on our cross-border exposures, but this is not a core driver of our Pillar 1 capital requirements.

items.title: Financial Policy Committee Record – July 2026
items.summary: The PRA/FPC Financial Policy Committee Record from June 2026 identifies vulnerabilities in risky asset valuations, sovereign debt, and credit markets (including private credit) as material risks to financial stability. Specific credit-related concerns include elevated leverage in equity markets, rapid AI-company credit market access across public and private credit channels, deteriorating asset quality in private credit funds, and complex debt structures creating opacity in credit risk measurement. The Committee emphasises interaction risks between these vulnerabilities and geopolitical shocks, alongside cyber/AI operational resilience impacts on credit institutions.
items.why_it_matters: Supervisory expectations for credit risk practitioners are shifting toward active monitoring of AI-driven credit concentration, private credit valuation/liquidity stress, and interconnected leverage dynamics across equity prime brokerage and sovereign debt markets.

quant.verdict: This FPC record directly shapes our model development agenda: we need enhanced PD calibration for AI-sector credit concentration, LGD stress testing for private credit funds under liquidity dislocation, and downturn scenarios linking leverage dynamics to asset quality deterioration—all of which supervisors will scrutinise closely in TRIM.

regulatory.verdict: This UK/PRA document flags supervisory attention to leverage concentration and private credit opacity, but my Nordic bank's corporate exposures are primarily traditional bank lending; I need to assess whether our AI-sector exposures, syndication exposures to private credit funds, or cross-border leverage correlations require active monitoring uplift, and whether ECB/Nordic FSAs are signalling similar expectations.

items.title: Regulatory Blog: EBA Simplification Package: Die EBA konkretisiert den Weg zum Pillar 3 DataHub
items.summary: The EBA's Simplification Package Module 8 proposes integrating Small and Non-Complex Institutions (SNCIs) into the Pillar 3 DataHub by deriving and publishing their disclosure data centrally from existing regulatory reporting (COREP/FINREP) rather than requiring separate submissions. For non-listed SNCIs, existing reporting data are sufficient; for listed SNCIs, limited reporting additions are proposed including two new OV1 data fields in COREP, a small FINREP module for credit quality data, and a remuneration block for affected institutions.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners at SNCIs will need to understand new proportionate reporting requirements for Pillar 3 disclosure, potential COREP and FINREP amendments, and the EBA's operational timeline for the central DataHub.

quant.verdict: This is a regulatory reporting and disclosure initiative for SNCIs that doesn't affect our PD/LGD model framework, calibration methodology, data governance for modelling purposes, or supervisory validation processes—we operate with full disclosure requirements regardless, so the simplification has no bearing on our model development or margin of conservatism assessment.

regulatory.verdict: I need to monitor the EBA Simplification Package Module 8 for any COREP/FINREP amendments affecting our corporate credit risk reporting, but the DataHub integration primarily impacts smaller institutions and our disclosure obligations rather than capital treatment of our Nordic corporate loan book.

items.title: AFME supports next steps for FPC review of bank capital requirements
items.summary: AFME supports the UK Financial Policy Committee's review of bank capital requirements, including planned reforms to the leverage ratio framework and removal of the Countercyclical Leverage Buffer. AFME advocates for simplification of the leverage ratio to align with international standards and enhance buffer useability. The FPC and PRA plan to consult on a package of capital framework reform measures.
items.why_it_matters: UK credit risk practitioners face potential changes to leverage ratio calibration, capital buffer frameworks, and regulatory alignment that could shift capital management and competitive positioning requirements.

quant.verdict: I care about leverage ratio mechanics only insofar as they interact with our IRB capital charge calculations and margin of conservatism assessment; this is pure leverage ratio policy advocacy with no direct PD/LGD modelling or data implication for our corporate exposures work.

regulatory.verdict: This UK-specific FPC leverage ratio reform is not material to my Nordic corporate loan book given our primary regulatory exposure to CRR3, ECB, and Nordic FSA frameworks, which operate under different leverage ratio architectures and buffer regimes.

items.title: ​The EBA publishes 2025 loss data for immovable property markets under Article 430a of the Capital Requirements Regulation
items.summary: The EBA published its 2025 annual dataset on losses and exposures for residential and commercial immovable property under Article 430a CRR, covering the EU/EEA. The data support application of preferential CRR treatments for immovable property exposures, including the loan-splitting approach (Articles 125–126) and IRB collateral recognition (Article 199). The publication provides harmonised supervisory data by national market to facilitate consistent application of loss-rate thresholds.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners can now use the EBA's centralized loss-rate data to determine eligibility for simplified treatments on residential and commercial real estate exposures under both standardised and IRB approaches.

quant.verdict: I need to incorporate the 2025 EBA Article 430a loss data into our IRB collateral models—this directly impacts LGD calibration for property-backed exposures, particularly for validating our downturn loss estimates and assessing margin of conservatism against supervisory benchmarks.

regulatory.verdict: I need to integrate the 2025 EBA Article 430a loss data into our immovable property risk framework immediately, as it directly governs whether we can apply preferential capital treatments (loan-splitting and IRB collateral recognition) to our Nordic real estate corporate exposures.

items.title: Regulatory Blog: EBA finalises its Disclosure Package
items.summary: The EBA published its Final Report on ITS for Pillar 3 disclosure covering ESG risks, equity exposures, and shadow banking entities (Step 2), amending Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2024/3172. The final ITS specifies requirements under CRR3 Articles 449a, 449b, and 438(e), with first reference dates of 31 December 2026 for large institutions and 31 December 2027 for small and non-complex institutions. Key changes include removal of GAR/BTAR and taxonomy-related templates 6–9, finalisation of equity exposure template EU CR 10.5 (with no breakdown required), and confirmation of a simplified approach for shadow banking entities requiring only aggregate on-/off-balance sheet exposure disclosure.
items.why_it_matters: You now have the final templates and implementation timelines for CRR3 Pillar 3 disclosure on ESG, equity, and shadow banking, with clarified phasing for large vs. small institutions and resolved inconsistencies from the first 2025 disclosure cycle.

quant.verdict: This is a disclosure and reporting matter with no direct bearing on PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or data infrastructure—the final templates and timelines are compliance/communication concerns for the disclosure team, not model governance.

regulatory.verdict: I need to integrate the final EBA Pillar 3 ITS into our CRR3 implementation roadmap immediately, confirm our disclosure categorisation (large institution status), and cascade the December 2026 first reference date requirements to our group financial reporting and regulatory teams, while noting that the removal of GAR/BTAR templates simplifies our taxonomy-related workload.

items.title: ISDA In Review – June 2026
items.summary: ISDA responded to RBI's consultation on draft amendment directions implementing the standardized approach for counterparty credit risk (SA-CCR). ISDA broadly welcomes the RBI's move to SA-CCR and updated capital treatment for exposures. This consultation addresses regulatory implementation of SA-CCR methodologies in the Indian banking sector.
items.why_it_matters: RBI's SA-CCR implementation will affect how Indian banks calculate counterparty credit risk capital, potentially influencing derivative hedging costs and cross-border exposure management for international banks interacting with Indian counterparties.

quant.verdict: This is a regulatory development in the Indian banking sector regarding counterparty credit risk capital (SA-CCR), which does not directly affect our corporate PD/LGD model frameworks, downturn calibration, data collection, or supervisory validation—our primary focus is IRB corporate exposures under the standardized approach to credit risk.

regulatory.verdict: While SA-CCR is a standard methodology we track, this item focuses exclusively on RBI implementation for Indian banking regulation, which has no bearing on our Nordic corporate loan book or CRR3 capital treatment obligations.

items.title: Floored: Basel III rethink upsets IRB dominance
items.summary: Risk.net commentary examines how the Basel III output floor is reducing the relative attractiveness of IRB approaches compared to standardised approaches. The output floor constrains the capital benefit banks can achieve through internal models, diminishing a key advantage of IRB adoption. The article suggests IRB models remain viable but face increased competitive pressure from simpler standardised methods.
items.why_it_matters: IRB-model advantages in capital efficiency are being compressed by the output floor, requiring practitioners to reassess model ROI and validate whether the compliance costs of IRB programmes remain justified.

quant.verdict: I must understand the Basel III output floor's interaction with our IRB capital outputs and reassess the marginal benefit of our model sophistication against the compliance burden; this directly shapes how conservatively we calibrate and whether we justify continued investment in model refinement.

regulatory.verdict: The Basel III output floor is a material constraint on our corporate IRB programme ROI and capital treatment strategy—I need to continuously monitor how floor impacts justify continued IRB compliance costs versus migrating segments to standardised approaches.

items.title: Prudential Regulation Authority Annual Report 2025/26
items.summary: The PRA Annual Report 2025/26 highlights key regulatory developments including finalisation of UK Basel 3.1 implementation and launch of the "Strong and Simple" framework for smaller firms. The report covers capital requirements, stress testing (2025 BCST and LIST with published firm-level results), and systemic resilience assessments. It addresses the PRA's primary objectives of safety, soundness and policyholder protection alongside secondary competition and growth objectives.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to understand the finalised Basel 3.1 framework implementation timelines, the "Strong and Simple" proportionality approach for smaller firms, and stress test expectations that will shape capital and provisioning requirements.

quant.verdict: I need to track the finalised UK Basel 3.1 implementation details and timelines closely, particularly any changes to IRB model recognition, output floors, and capital treatment that will directly impact our PD/LGD calibration scope and capital requirement calculations; the "Strong and Simple" framework implications for our smaller corporate exposures also warrant immediate assessment for any proportionality adjustments to validation or downturn estimation requirements.

regulatory.verdict: This is a UK PRA report with no direct bearing on Nordic corporate credit risk or CRR3 implementation in my jurisdiction—our regulatory regime is ECB/EBA-driven, not PRA-governed.

items.title: Targeted EBA peer review finds high compliance on Pillar 3 disclosures but calls for greater consistency
items.summary: The EBA published results of a targeted peer review on supervisory compliance with CRR Pillar 3 disclosure requirements across the EU covering June 2023 to June 2025. The review found most competent authorities have fully or largely embedded Pillar 3 requirements into their supervisory frameworks, though consistency across jurisdictions varies. One authority received "not applied" ratings due to absence of formal methodologies, while most others demonstrated high convergence in supervisory practices.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to understand supervisory expectations and variations in Pillar 3 disclosure enforcement across jurisdictions, which may affect reporting requirements and supervisory scrutiny of their institutions.

quant.verdict: While Pillar 3 disclosure compliance is a supervisory reporting requirement, this peer review finding has no direct implication for my PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or downturn estimation work—it addresses supervisory oversight of disclosure practices rather than model methodology or data requirements.

regulatory.verdict: I need to monitor the EBA's consistency findings closely, but this peer review itself has limited direct impact on our Nordic corporate loan book—the real value lies in tracking how our ECB supervisor and Nordic FSAs translate these expectations into our specific Pillar 3 supervisory requirements and reporting timelines.

items.title: Claudia Buch: Hearing of the Committee on Economic and Monetary Affairs of the European Parliament
items.summary: ECB Supervisory Board Chair Claudia Buch addresses European Parliament on banking sector resilience amid geopolitical risks and AI challenges. She emphasizes that capital requirements remain essential for banks to absorb shocks and support lending, with euro area banks maintaining strong capitalization and low NPLs. The speech highlights supervisory priorities including operational resilience under DORA, cyber risk management, and the need for banks to assess credit risk impacts from geopolitical developments and structural changes to borrower conditions and collateral values.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to understand ECB supervisory expectations on capital adequacy, asset quality monitoring under geopolitical stress, and enhanced operational resilience requirements affecting banks' loss-absorbing capacity assessment.

quant.verdict: I need to track this closely: ECB chair statement on supervisory expectations regarding capital adequacy, credit risk assessment under geopolitical stress, and operational resilience directly shapes our model validation framework, downturn scenario calibration, and TRIM engagement priorities.

regulatory.verdict: I need to track ECB Supervisory Board messaging on capital adequacy and credit risk assessment methodologies, particularly the emphasis on geopolitical stress testing and operational resilience under DORA as these directly shape our expected supervisory dialogue and capital management framework for corporate exposures.

items.title: Regulatory Blog: EBA Simplification Package: ESG‑Risiken im Meldewesen verankert - Neues EBA‑Modul für alle Institute
items.summary: The EBA has developed a harmonized ESG reporting module (Module 7) as part of CRR3 implementation under Article 430(1)(h) CRR, which establishes mandatory reporting on environmental, climate, and ESG risks for all institutions. The module implements a three-tier proportional approach based on institution size and type, with large institutions reporting seven templates, other capital-market-oriented institutions six templates, and SNCIs/smaller non-capital-market institutions reporting a single consolidated template. The new permanent ESG reporting framework consolidates previous ad-hoc data collections and aligns closely with Pillar 3 disclosure templates and CSRD/ESRS logic to minimize duplicate reporting.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must prepare for substantial operational changes to supervisory reporting processes and data governance, including integration of ESG data across FINREP, stress testing, and disclosure systems, with compliance timelines dependent on institution classification and CRR3 transposition dates.

quant.verdict: Mandatory ESG reporting is an operational and compliance matter for my institution, but it has no direct implication for PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or downturn estimation under IRB—the core technical work I do.

regulatory.verdict: I need to track this EBA Module 7 ESG reporting framework closely as it directly impacts our supervisory reporting obligations under CRR3 Article 430(1)(h), requiring immediate classification of our Nordic corporate exposures portfolio and assessment of our data governance maturity against the three-tier proportionality structure.

items.title: ECB sanctions BIL for breaching ECB decision on internal models
items.summary: The ECB imposed a €3.255 million penalty on Banque Internationale à Luxembourg for intentionally failing to apply approved internal models to calculate expected loss for retail and corporate exposures in default for one year. As a result, BIL failed to properly calculate the internal ratings-based shortfall, overstating its capital ratios for three consecutive quarters (Q4 2023–Q2 2024). The breach was classified as "severe" under the ECB's penalty framework.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must understand that failure to properly implement ECB-approved IRB models for expected loss calculation directly translates to capital misstatement and supervisory enforcement; this case illustrates the high standard of compliance required and the severity classification that triggers substantial penalties.

quant.verdict: This is a material cautionary case demonstrating that supervisory enforcement extends beyond model conceptual soundness to rigorous implementation and application discipline; as someone responsible for IRB model governance, I need to ensure our controls architecture prevents intentional or systematic failures in model-to-portfolio application logic and expected loss calculation, and that our three-lines-of-defense can catch such breaches before they inflate capital ratios across multiple reporting periods.

regulatory.verdict: I must ensure our retail and corporate IRB model governance strictly enforces quarterly expected loss calculations under approved models—BIL's €3.255M penalty for one-year non-compliance and capital misstatement across three quarters demonstrates that ECB treats model implementation failures as severe violations with material enforcement consequences for Nordic banks operating under comparable IRB regimes.

items.title: The EBA publishes its 2025 Report on supervisory convergence, supporting a simpler and more efficient EU prudential framework
items.summary: The EBA published its 2025 Report on supervisory convergence, highlighting progress in aligning supervisory practices across the EU and identifying areas needing further alignment. Key developments include work on Basel III implementation, CRR3 implementation, resilience testing, and SREP convergence. The EBA will continue deepening convergence in 2026 with focus on Basel III reforms, resolution testing, and strengthened supervisory oversight.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to track supervisory convergence priorities and Basel III/CRR3 implementation timelines, as these directly influence capital model validation, SREP expectations, and prudential framework application across EU jurisdictions.

quant.verdict: The EBA convergence report is essential tracking material for our IRB model validation strategy and SREP engagement, particularly as Basel III reforms and CRR3 implementation reshape supervisory expectations around capital model standards and model governance across EU jurisdictions.

regulatory.verdict: I need to track this report closely as it sets the EBA's supervisory convergence roadmap for 2025-2026, which will directly shape our SREP dialogue, CRR3 implementation sequencing, and how our capital models are validated across Nordic FSA jurisdictions.

items.title: ECB sanctions BIL for breaching ECB decision on internal models
items.summary: The ECB imposed a €3.255 million administrative penalty on Banque Internationale à Luxembourg for failing to apply its approved internal models to calculate expected loss for retail and corporate exposures, resulting in understatement of the internal ratings-based shortfall and overstatement of capital for three consecutive quarters (Q4 2023–Q2 2024). The breach was classified as "severe" because the bank reported higher capital ratios than warranted, misrepresenting its capital strength and ability to absorb losses. The bank has the right to challenge the decision before the Court of Justice of the European Union.
items.why_it_matters: This enforcement action confirms the ECB's strict interpretation of IRB model governance requirements and that internal models must be applied as approved; failures to calculate expected loss shortcuts directly expose banks to supervisory sanction and reputational risk.

quant.verdict: This sanction directly impacts how we must operationalize our PD/LGD models—the ECB's enforcement confirms that approved models must be applied consistently without shortcuts, and any deviation in expected loss calculation carries severe penalties and supervisory scrutiny that will shape our validation and governance protocols going forward.

regulatory.verdict: I must ensure our corporate and retail IRB models are applied consistently and as approved without exception, as the ECB treats model application failures as severe governance breaches that trigger substantial penalties and reputational damage.

items.title: ECB streamlines supervisory guidance to improve clarity and transparency
items.summary: The ECB is conducting a comprehensive review of approximately 130 supervisory guidance publications, discontinuing around 40 outdated documents and revising others including guides on internal capital adequacy assessment, internal models, credit risk data aggregation, and licence applications. The review updates classification of supervisory publications to emphasise their non-binding nature and reflects recent regulatory developments including CRR III rules on credit valuation adjustment and forthcoming EBA guidelines on credit conversion factors and internal governance.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to track which supervisory expectations remain active and understand clarified guidance on capital adequacy assessment, internal model methodologies, and risk data requirements that inform IRB/standardised approach implementation.

quant.verdict: I need to monitor which of the 130 ECB guidance documents affect my PD/LGD model framework, particularly any revisions to internal model methodology expectations, data aggregation standards, and capital adequacy assessment criteria that could influence our model validation protocols and supervisory submissions.

regulatory.verdict: I need to systematically identify which of the 40 discontinued guidance documents previously informed our corporate IRB models and capital framework, and flag any changes to active guidance on ICAAP, internal models, and credit risk data aggregation that could trigger model recalibrations or control enhancements.

items.title: ECB streamlines supervisory guidance to improve clarity and transparency
items.summary: ECB is conducting a comprehensive review of approximately 130 supervisory guidance publications covering guides, reports, letters and methodologies. Around 40 outdated or superseded documents are being discontinued, while others are being revised or substantially updated to reflect recent regulatory developments including CRR III, EBA guidelines, and supervisory feedback. Key revisions include updates to guidance on management buffers, credit conversion factors, credit valuation adjustment, governance, licence applications, risk data aggregation, on-site inspections, and leveraged transactions.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must track these revisions to supervisory expectations as they clarify how the ECB interprets capital requirements, internal models, and credit risk mitigation practices under CRR III and EBA standards.

quant.verdict: This ECB guidance consolidation is directly material to our IRB models: clarifications on credit conversion factors, management buffers, credit risk mitigation, and supervisory expectations for internal models will likely reshape how we calibrate PD/LGD assumptions, set downturn estimates, and prepare for TRIM reviews under the updated CRR III framework.

regulatory.verdict: I need to systematically track this ECB guidance review as it directly shapes how we interpret capital requirements and credit risk treatment for our Nordic corporate portfolio under CRR III, particularly on management buffers, credit conversion factors, and leveraged transactions which are material to our exposures.

items.title: ECB concludes asset quality reviews of KfW Beteiligungsholding GmbH and Promontoria 19 Coöperatie U.A.
items.summary: The ECB completed asset quality reviews (AQRs) of two recently significant banks: KfW Beteiligungsholding GmbH and Promontoria 19 Coöperatie U.A., focusing on credit risk across their major portfolios. The reviews resulted in CET1 capital ratio depletions of 87 and 400 basis points respectively, but neither bank fell below its capital requirements. The ECB expects the banks to implement supervisory measures addressing identified weaknesses.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to track capital impacts and supervisory follow-up measures for these significant banks, and understand how asset quality findings influence ongoing prudential supervision and SREP assessments.

quant.verdict: The ECB AQR outcomes for KfW and Promontoria 19 signal supervisory findings on credit risk classification and provisioning practices, which is relevant for understanding how regulators assess model adequacy—but without visibility into the specific model deficiencies identified, I cannot determine whether this requires changes to our own PD/LGD calibration frameworks or margin of conservatism assumptions.

regulatory.verdict: While AQR rigor is relevant to supervisory expectations, these two banks operate outside the Nordic corporate loan book I manage, so their specific capital depletion outcomes and remedial measures have no direct bearing on my regulatory capital treatment decisions or peer bank comparisons.

items.title: Regulatory Blog: EBA Simplification Package: Mehr Transparenz bei Eigenmitteln und RWA: Die geplanten COREP Änderungen der EBA im Fokus
items.summary: PwC analysis of EBA's Simplification Package proposals for COREP capital reporting under CRR3, focusing on new transition rule templates (C 05.03), enhanced capital ratio templates (C 03.00, C 04.00), output floor adjustments, and standardised credit risk template (C 07.00) changes. The proposals aim to improve transparency on capital requirements, RWA, and transition provisions while reducing double reporting and data gaps.
items.why_it_matters: Banks must prepare for significant changes to capital reporting templates and supervisory transparency on CRR3 transitions and output floor impacts, with particular operational complexity for large IRB institutions managing data governance and reconciliation processes.

quant.verdict: This directly impacts our IRB model governance and TRIM-readiness: the new C 05.03 transition templates and C 07.00 standardised credit risk template will require us to systematically decompose output floor impacts and RWA floor effects by exposure class, forcing tighter reconciliation between internal models and standardised approaches—critical for demonstrating model conservatism and validating our transition compliance to supervisors.

regulatory.verdict: I need to track the EBA's COREP simplification proposals closely, as these directly impact our corporate credit risk reporting obligations and capital transparency requirements under CRR3—particularly the transition rules (C 05.03) and standardised credit risk template changes (C 07.00), which will affect how we report our Nordic corporate exposures and RWA calculations.

items.title: ECB concludes asset quality reviews of KfW Beteiligungsholding GmbH and Promontoria 19 Coöperatie U.A.
items.summary: The ECB completed asset quality reviews (AQRs) of two newly significant banks—KfW IPEX and Promontoria—focusing on credit risk across their portfolios (>80% of RWAs). The reviews identified AQR-adjusted CET1 ratio depletions of 87 and 400 basis points respectively, but neither bank falls below capital requirements. Results will inform ongoing supervision and the Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must understand the supervisory findings and capital impacts on these significant institutions, which will shape their ongoing prudential supervision and may influence risk management and capital planning expectations.

quant.verdict: While AQR outcomes are important for the specific institutions, this announcement has limited direct relevance to my PD/LGD model development and validation work—it's primarily a supervisory disclosure of findings rather than guidance on methodology, data standards, or regulatory expectations that would affect our modelling practices.

regulatory.verdict: While AQR findings on capital impacts are supervisory milestones, neither KfW nor Promontoria competes materially in our Nordic corporate loan book, and their AQR conclusions don't establish precedent for our own capital or risk framework.

items.title: Patrick Montagner: When fragmentation creates complexity
items.summary: ECB supervisory board member Patrick Montagner discusses regulatory complexity and fragmentation in EU banking supervision, arguing complexity reflects institutional reality rather than being inherently problematic. He identifies fragmentation and lack of integration as the genuine issue and proposes tailoring supervisory intensity through proportionality, including potentially raising the €5 billion threshold for small and non-complex institutions (SNCIs).
items.why_it_matters: ECB supervisory leadership signals openness to reducing reporting burdens on smaller banks and potentially expanding SNCI thresholds, which may reduce compliance costs but require credit risk teams to track evolution of proportionality rules.

quant.verdict: I need to monitor ECB proportionality policy evolution carefully—threshold changes for SNCIs could shift which clients fall under full IRB vs. simplified regimes, affecting our segmentation strategy and model scope, but this is still at the signal/discussion phase rather than regulatory proposal.

regulatory.verdict: I need to monitor Montagner's proportionality advocacy carefully—any ECB move to expand SNCI thresholds or redefine supervisory scope could shift our own classification and reporting obligations, but as a major Nordic bank we're unlikely to benefit from SNCIs relief; however, the fragmentation concerns he raises are directly relevant to how CRR3 and its divergent national implementation will affect our corporate exposures across Scandinavia.

items.title: Regulatory Blog: Kommission nimmt FRTB-Rechtsakt ohne wesentliche Anpassungen an und veröffentlicht Q&A zu Auslegungsfragen
items.summary: The EU Commission adopted the FRTB regulatory act on 4 June 2026 largely unchanged from the April draft, and published Q&A clarifying implementation issues. Key clarifications include: trading book boundary under CRR2 (Basel 2.5) will apply throughout the three-year transition period (not FRTB boundary from 1 January 2027); the bank-specific multiplier applies to both unfloored (U-TREA) and floored (S-TREA) capital requirements to ensure capital neutrality against the output floor; and enhanced reporting/disclosure requirements for multiplier users remain in force.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners using the bank-specific multiplier now have clarity that they may apply a single CRR2 trading book boundary throughout the transition and that the multiplier will shield them from output-floor creep, but must continue dual reporting under both FRTB and pre-FRTB (CRR as of 8 July 2024) frameworks.

quant.verdict: This is a market risk (FRTB) regulatory clarification with no direct bearing on my PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or downturn estimation work in the corporate IRB space.

regulatory.verdict: I need to flag that this item is primarily about trading book treatment under FRTB, which falls outside my corporate credit risk mandate—but the multiplier mechanics and output floor interaction merit watching for any spillover into standardised approaches for corporates, and I should monitor EBA Q&As for corporate-specific clarifications.

items.title: Sharon Donnery: Interview with Central Banking
items.summary: ECB Supervisory Board member Sharon Donnery discusses key supervisory priorities including geopolitical risk resilience, timely remediation of outstanding issues, and digital transformation/AI strategies. The ECB is conducting a 2026 thematic reverse stress test focused on geopolitical risk to assess bank exposures and recovery capabilities across different business models. Results will be published in summer 2026 with qualitative feedback to individual banks through Joint Supervisory Teams.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners should understand ECB supervisory expectations on stress testing methodology for geopolitical scenarios, risk data aggregation remediation timelines, and the shift towards bank-specific reverse stress testing rather than standard scenarios.

quant.verdict: The ECB's 2026 geopolitical reverse stress test signals a critical supervisory focus that will directly shape our downturn estimation methodology, scenario design, and data aggregation requirements—we need to align our PD/LGD stress testing frameworks and recovery curve assumptions with the ECB's stated expectations before results are published.

regulatory.verdict: I need to track the 2026 geopolitical reverse stress test timeline and methodology closely, as it will directly impact our capital framework assumptions and may require material changes to our corporate credit risk modelling and data aggregation infrastructure by 2025.

items.title: Frank Elderson: Fireside chat
items.summary: ECB Vice-Chair Elderson addresses regulatory simplification at the Spanish Banking Association, emphasizing the distinction between simplification (which maintains resilience) and deregulation (which weakens it). The SSM reports tangible progress on reducing supervisory burden: 80% of simple capital decisions approved within one week (vs. months previously), securitisation approvals reduced from three months to under ten working days, and stress-test reporting requirements cut by 55% through aligned templates. The speech stresses that competitiveness depends on banking market integration and completing the banking union rather than weakening prudential standards.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners should understand the SSM's explicit commitment to streamlining supervisory processes and reporting without lowering capital requirements, and the supervisory expectation that real competitiveness gains depend on market integration, not regulatory relief.

quant.verdict: Useful context on SSM's operational efficiency efforts—particularly the stress-test template harmonisation (55% reduction) and capital decision timelines—but this is primarily a supervisory process message without material implications for PD/LGD model development, calibration, or validation frameworks.

regulatory.verdict: I note Elderson's message that SSM is pursuing genuine operational efficiency—faster approval timelines and cleaner reporting templates—but I'm reassured by the explicit statement that simplification preserves resilience standards and won't erode our capital requirements; this informs how we communicate with supervisors on burden reduction requests and calibrates my expectations for ECB engagement on our own regulatory submissions.

items.title: ECB publishes supervisory banking statistics on significant institutions for the first quarter of 2026
items.summary: The ECB published Q1 2026 supervisory banking statistics for significant institutions showing: CET1 ratio at 15.99% (down from 16.26%), non-performing loans ratio at 2.18% (unchanged), liquidity coverage ratio at 153.93% (down from 158.60%), and net stable funding ratio at 125.63% (lowest since 2021). The release includes new breakdowns of liquidity buffers and detailed metrics by counterparty sector and country.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need these supervisory statistics to benchmark capital adequacy, asset quality and provisioning trends across SSM-supervised banks and assess supervisory expectations for minimum capital and liquidity requirements.

quant.verdict: I should monitor this release for peer institution credit quality trends and provisioning adequacy, but the supervisory aggregates themselves won't directly reshape my PD/LGD model assumptions—the new liquidity buffer breakdowns and sector/country detail are useful for context, however benchmarking against published ratios is less material to model calibration than internal portfolio-level default and recovery data.

regulatory.verdict: I need to track these Q1 2026 supervisory benchmarks closely—the CET1 decline, NSFR hitting its lowest since 2021, and new liquidity breakdowns directly inform our capital modeling, stress-testing assumptions, and how ECB supervisors will likely calibrate their requirements for our corporate book.

items.title: PRA sets out adjustments to its market risk internal model approach under Basel 3.1
items.summary: The PRA has published a consultation on adjustments to the internal model approach (IMA) for market risk under Basel 3.1, the final piece of Basel 3.1 implementation in the UK. The proposals include extending the P&L attribution test monitoring period from one to three years, adjusting treatment of limited trading data, reducing barriers to IMA transition, and operational simplifications. Implementation is scheduled for 1 January 2028, with other Basel 3.1 rules entering force in January 2027.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to monitor Basel 3.1 capital framework deployment; these market risk IMA adjustments complete the UK's Basel 3.1 roadmap and may influence capital planning for trading-exposed firms or multi-jurisdictional institutions.

quant.verdict: This is a market risk IMA consultation that does not affect PD/LGD model development, calibration, or validation—my primary concern as a credit risk modeller; while Basel 3.1 capital rules matter for overall framework oversight, this specific item addresses trading book mechanics rather than credit risk measurement and downturn estimation.

regulatory.verdict: This is a PRA market risk IMA consultation for trading book capital treatment under Basel 3.1; while Basel 3.1 matters to us strategically, our corporate loan book exposure sits in the credit risk pillar with standardised or IRB approaches, and market risk IMA is not our operational concern.

items.title: ECB publishes supervisory banking statistics on significant institutions for the first quarter of 2026
items.summary: ECB publishes Q1 2026 supervisory banking statistics for significant institutions showing CET1 ratio at 15.99% (down from 16.26%), NPL ratio at 2.18% (unchanged), and net stable funding ratio at 125.63% (lowest since 2021). Key metrics show slight deterioration in capital adequacy and funding stability, with liquidity coverage ratio declining to 153.93%.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to track supervisory capital adequacy and asset quality trends across the ECB-supervised banking sector to assess systemic resilience and inform stress testing and policy calibration.

quant.verdict: I track ECB regulatory developments closely, but aggregate sector-level statistics on capital, NPLs, and liquidity ratios have no direct bearing on my PD/LGD model development, calibration, or downturn estimation work for corporate exposures.

regulatory.verdict: This is aggregated industry benchmarking data for significant institutions across the ECB-supervised perimeter; it does not contain capital requirements, CRR3 implementation mandates, supervisory guidance, or reporting obligations that directly affect how my bank structures corporate exposures or capital treatment.

items.title: ISDA, SIFMA, IIF Respond to 2026 US G-SIB Surcharge Proposal
items.summary: ISDA, SIFMA, and IIF submitted joint comments on the 2026 US G-SIB surcharge proposal, welcoming improvements on client-cleared derivatives treatment but recommending further refinements. Key recommendations include: excluding derivatives from the cross-jurisdictional activity indicator, averaging OTC derivative notional amounts using monthly rather than daily values, reporting cross-jurisdictional claims quarterly rather than monthly, further consideration for cross-product netting, and excluding the standardized approach CCR alpha factor from interconnectedness measures.
items.why_it_matters: The proposed changes to G-SIB surcharge methodologies directly affect capital requirements for systemically important banks, particularly those engaged in derivatives clearing and trading, with implications for counterparty credit risk measurement and reporting frequency.

quant.verdict: This is a capital surcharge advocacy item with no implications for my PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or IRB data infrastructure—the surcharge methodology operates orthogonally to probability of default and loss-given-default estimation.

regulatory.verdict: This is a US G-SIB surcharge proposal with no direct bearing on my Nordic corporate loan book or CRR3 implementation; while it affects derivative treatment at systemically important institutions, it does not influence capital treatment of Scandinavian corporates or ECB/Nordic FSA supervisory expectations for corporate exposures.

items.title: Eyeing the Basel III Finish Line
items.summary: ISDA publishes analysis of the revised US Basel III endgame proposal (March 2026), showing material improvements in market risk capital calibration: FRTB standardized approach impact reduced from 101% to 89%, and internal models approach from 73% to 30%. ISDA identifies remaining issues requiring fine-tuning, including cross-product netting methodology under SA-CCR, FRTB default risk charge, and credit valuation adjustment risk capital treatment.
items.why_it_matters: US banks and international groups must track outstanding calibration concerns in the finalizing Basel III endgame, particularly on CCR netting conservatism and FRTB components, which will directly affect capital requirement calculations and internal model viability.

quant.verdict: This is primarily a trading/treasury capital concern with limited direct impact on my PD/LGD credit model work, though I need to monitor final Basel III calibrations that could reshape IRB portfolio treatment and downturn estimation assumptions if they alter supervisory risk weighting frameworks downstream.

regulatory.verdict: This US Basel III endgame analysis is not material to my Nordic corporate loan book - my capital concerns center on CRR3 implementation timelines and ECB/Nordic FSA supervisory expectations, not US regulatory outcomes or market risk framework changes that predominantly affect trading book exposures.

items.title: IIF, ISDA and SIFMA Submit Comment Letter on Basel III Endgame Proposal
items.summary: Industry associations (IIF, ISDA, SIFMA) submitted a joint comment letter to US banking regulators on the proposed Basel III endgame capital rule, addressing Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB), credit valuation adjustment (CVA) risk, and counterparty credit risk (CCR) frameworks. The letter emphasizes risk sensitivity, consistency across capital rules, and operational efficiency, recommending an implementation effective date no earlier than January 1, 2028.
items.why_it_matters: The comment letter signals industry concerns and recommendations on the calibration of FRTB, CVA, and SA-CCR rules that will directly affect capital requirements for large banking organizations' trading and derivatives activities.

quant.verdict: While Basel III Endgame will eventually affect our market risk capital rules, this industry comment letter on trading book and CVA frameworks has limited direct bearing on my PD/LGD model work for corporate credit exposures under the IRB approach.

regulatory.verdict: I monitor this for tracking US-CRR3 alignment, but as a Nordic corporate lender focused on non-trading book exposures, FRTB and CVA refinements in the US endgame rule are outside my direct remit; our trading desks and group regulatory affairs will own this.

items.title: ISDA, SIFMA, IIF Respond to 2026 US Basel III Proposal
items.summary: ISDA, SIFMA, and IIF submitted a joint response to the 2026 US Basel III notice of proposed rulemaking, focusing on the Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB), credit valuation adjustment (CVA) framework, securities financing transactions, and the standardized approach for counterparty credit risk (SA-CCR). The industry quantitative impact study indicates the proposed FRTB framework would increase capital requirements by 30–89% versus current US standardized approach for market risk. The associations welcome recalibrations from the 2023 NPR that reduce capital impact and propose additional targeted adjustments to improve risk sensitivity.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must track US Basel III implementation timelines and SA-CCR recalibration proposals, as the capital impact ranges and industry feedback will shape final rules affecting counterparty credit risk and trading book capital requirements.

quant.verdict: While Basel III implementation has eventual supervisory importance, this item focuses on trading book market risk (FRTB) and CVA capital frameworks that sit outside my PD/LGD model scope; my mandate is corporate IRB, not trading or counterparty credit risk modeling.

regulatory.verdict: This is primarily a US regulatory matter focused on trading book and CVA frameworks; my Nordic bank's corporate loan book is unaffected by US Basel III FRTB implementation or SA-CCR recalibration timelines, so I will not allocate monitoring resources here.

items.title: Regulatory Blog: EBA Simplification Package: Marktrisiko-Reporting - Proportionalität im Fokus
items.summary: The EBA published Consultation Paper EBA/CP/2026/007 on 10 April 2026 proposing updates to COREP market risk reporting templates as part of FRTB implementation and the EBA simplification agenda. The proposals cover trading book boundary and thresholds, market risk approaches (SSA, ASA, AIMA), proportionality relief for small trading books, and reporting on CCR, CVA, and prudent valuation. Key changes include exempting small trading book institutions from certain templates and streamlining dimensional breakdowns to reduce reporting burden.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to understand revised market risk reporting requirements and proportionality carve-outs under FRTB implementation, particularly exemptions for small trading books and simplified templates affecting their institution's prudential compliance calendar.

quant.verdict: This is a market risk and prudential reporting matter that sits outside my remit as a credit risk modeller; while proportionality relief may affect our compliance calendar, it has no bearing on PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or IRB supervision.

regulatory.verdict: I need to focus on trading book boundary and capital treatment impacts if they affect corporate exposures, but market risk COREP reporting itself—even with simplifications—falls outside my remit as a corporate credit risk regulatory affairs specialist in a Nordic bank.

items.title: EBF White Paper on Specialised Lending
items.summary: The EBF White Paper argues that Basel 4/CRR3 prudential rules for specialised lending (project finance, object finance, commodity finance, income-producing real estate) are overly conservative, with LGD floors penalising low-risk portfolios where 71% of realised losses in project finance fall below the 25% regulatory floor. The paper calls for urgent recalibration: flexible modelling for low-default portfolios, lower LGD input floors, improved collateral recognition, review of the Slotting Approach, lower risk weights under the Standardised Approach, and permanent transitional arrangements for unrated corporates and a frozen output floor.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must assess whether proposed changes to LGD floors, risk weights, collateral treatment, and output floor mechanics will reshape capital requirements for specialised lending portfolios and influence future CRR3 calibration decisions.

quant.verdict: This is directly material to my PD/LGD modelling agenda: the EBF's empirical challenge to LGD floors (71% of project finance losses below 25%) cuts to the heart of downturn calibration and margin of conservatism decisions I must justify in model validation and TRIM reviews—their push for lower input floors and flexible modelling for low-default portfolios would reshape our IRB parameter settings, collateral treatment, and supervisory discussions around whether current floors are empirically defensible or punitive.

regulatory.verdict: I need to closely monitor EBF's specialised lending advocacy on LGD floors and output floor mechanics, as these directly shape our CRR3 capital treatment for project and commodity finance exposures in our Nordic corporate book, and we should consider a coordinated response if the proposals gain traction with the EBA or ECB.

items.title: Digital Assets and Derivatives: Where Next?
items.summary: ISDA paper examines institutional integration of digital assets into derivatives markets through the lens of settlement design, prudential capital treatment, and collateral management. The analysis demonstrates that faster settlement and portfolio compression can reduce counterparty exposure and XVA by 40–45%, and concludes that digital assets can scale within existing regulatory regimes if structured with appropriate settlement finality and collateral arrangements qualifying for regulatory capital treatment commensurate with underlying asset risk.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must understand how digital asset settlement mechanics interact with counterparty credit risk capital and liquidity frameworks, particularly regarding exposure persistence, mark-to-market timing, and collateral treatment under prudential standards.

quant.verdict: This addresses derivatives infrastructure and XVA optimization, which are treasury and counterparty credit management concerns, not core to PD/LGD model development, calibration, or downturn estimation for corporate credit exposures under IRB.

regulatory.verdict: While this ISDA analysis on digital asset settlement and derivatives counterparty credit risk is technically sound, it has limited bearing on my core mandate around Nordic corporate loan exposures—our primary concern is CRR3 implementation, EBA guidance, and supervisory expectations for the corporates we lend to, not the infrastructure of our derivatives trading book or collateral optimization across institutional counterparties.

items.title: ISDA Publishes ISDA SIMM® Methodology, Version 2.8+2512
items.summary: ISDA has published SIMM® Methodology version 2.8+2512 following the 2026 primary calibration exercise using historical data through 31 December 2025. The new version becomes effective 11 July 2026 for calculating initial margin, with first exchanges occurring 13 July 2026. This update reflects a full recalibration of the standardized initial margin model.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners using SIMM for initial margin calculation must transition to v2.8+2512 by 11 July 2026 and update their IM models and systems accordingly.

quant.verdict: SIMM is a counterparty risk and collateral management tool for OTC derivatives initial margin, not a PD/LGD model component—it has no bearing on my IRB credit risk models, calibration datasets, or downturn adjustments.

regulatory.verdict: I'm not responsible for derivatives initial margin methodology—this is a trading/treasury matter outside my corporate credit risk remit and has no bearing on Nordic corporate loan capital treatment or supervisory reporting.

items.title: Regulatory Blog: EBA Simplification Package: FINREP im Wandel der Zeit
items.summary: The EBA is comprehensively revising FINREP (Financial Reporting) under its simplification package, primarily to implement IFRS 18 (effective 1 January 2027), enhance regulatory data requirements, and reduce reporting costs. Key changes include restructuring the P&L statement (template F 02.00) to reflect operating/investing/financing categories, deleting or streamlining redundant templates, introducing proportionality measures for smaller institutions (SNCIs), and enhancing collateral data granularity with new templates F 37.00 and F 48.00.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must prepare for material changes to supervisory reporting structures and templates from 2027 onwards, including new collateral reporting requirements and potential reallocation of disclosure obligations between FINREP and other reporting frameworks.

quant.verdict: While the EBA simplification package will reshape supervisory reporting templates from 2027 onwards, this primarily affects the FINREP disclosure and operational reporting function—not our PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation or downturn estimation processes.

regulatory.verdict: I need to monitor EBA's FINREP simplification timeline closely for 2027 implementation, but the credit risk impact is secondary to finance and reporting teams—our exposure book architecture won't change, though collateral data templates (F 37.00, F 48.00) warrant review for potential impact on our corporate lending documentation and regulatory reporting workflows.

items.title: Frank Elderson: Interview with Het Financieele Dagblad
items.summary: ECB Vice-Chair Frank Elderson discusses supervisory simplification initiatives, including risk-prioritisation in annual reviews, streamlining of nearly 100 supervisory guidance publications, and acceleration of securitisation approvals from three months to seven days. He emphasizes capital requirements remain stable and appropriate, rejecting deregulation while supporting integration and completion of the banking union. The interview contextualizes ECB supervisory philosophy on capital adequacy, governance risk oversight, and administrative efficiency improvements.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to understand ECB's new risk-prioritisation review cycle, which reduces frequency of certain risk assessments for low-finding institutions, and the consolidation of supervisory guidance that will reshape compliance and reporting expectations.

quant.verdict: I need to track the consolidation of supervisory guidance and any changes to review cycle frequency that could affect my model validation timelines and TRIM preparation, but the item lacks specifics on whether guidance changes touch PD/LGD methodologies, data requirements, or calibration standards—without those details, the operational impact is unclear.

regulatory.verdict: I need to track these ECB supervisory simplification initiatives closely—the streamlining of 100+ guidance publications will directly impact our compliance and reporting roadmap, and the accelerated securitisation approval timeline (three months to seven days) is material for our capital markets operations and corporate exposure management.

items.title: Claudia Buch: Interview with Les Echos
items.summary: ECB Supervisory Board Chair Claudia Buch discusses supervisory priorities including geopolitical risks' implications for credit risk, cyber resilience monitoring, and proportionality reforms in supervision particularly around reporting and regulatory complexity. The ECB is assessing how energy prices, tariffs, and supply chain disruptions affect banks' credit risk and working to simplify supervisory guidance while maintaining prudential standards.
items.why_it_matters: Supervisors are intensifying focus on credit risk assessment under geopolitical shocks and signalling proportionality reviews of reporting requirements, which may affect practitioners' compliance scope and supervisory expectations on risk measurement.

quant.verdict: Supervisory messaging on geopolitical impact on credit risk merits attention for downturn estimation and scenario design, but the proportionality reforms and reporting simplification signal no imminent model-specific changes to IRB frameworks or data requirements.

regulatory.verdict: I need to track ECB supervisory priorities on credit risk assessment under geopolitical stress and monitor the proportionality review initiative, as changes to reporting requirements and risk measurement expectations could directly impact our Nordic corporate loan portfolio compliance framework and capital model validations.

items.title: ISDA In Review – May 2026
items.summary: ISDA published responses to regulatory consultations including the RBI's standardized approach for counterparty credit risk (SA-CCR) implementation, which ISDA broadly welcomes. The item also references ISDA comment letters on swap dealer thresholds and OTC derivatives market statistics, indicating ongoing supervisory engagement on derivatives capital treatment.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need visibility on SA-CCR implementation timelines and supervisory positions, and ISDA's responses signal how counterparty credit risk frameworks are evolving across jurisdictions.

quant.verdict: SA-CCR is a counterparty credit risk framework; it doesn't directly affect my PD/LGD model development, calibration or downturn estimation for corporate exposures under IRB.

regulatory.verdict: SA-CCR and derivatives capital treatment are not material to my corporate loan book – this is counterparty credit risk and dealer regulation territory, which sits outside our Nordic corporate exposures mandate.

items.title: ISDA Responds to BoE Consultation on Permissions to Facilitate Mobilization of New CCPs
items.summary: ISDA submitted a response to the Bank of England's consultation on using permissions and requirements powers to facilitate mobilization of new central counterparties (CCPs), including provisions for de minimis limits and rule waivers during a mobilization phase. ISDA supports the framework while recommending extension to existing CCPs, clearer safeguards, transparent de minimis limits, strong disclosure to clearing members, and strict constraints on rule waivers. The consultation addresses CCP governance and operational flexibility for new market infrastructure.
items.why_it_matters: Changes to CCP permissions and capital relief frameworks under BoE oversight may affect counterparty credit risk capital treatment and collateral mobilization rules for UK credit institutions.
