items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners involved in securitisation need to understand the PRA's supervisory expectations and discretionary powers when imposing Article 244, 245, 254 and 258 requirements on their firm.

quant.verdict: This SoP addresses securitisation regulatory powers but has no bearing on my PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or downturn estimation work for corporate exposures under the IRB approach.

regulatory.verdict: I do not engage with PRA securitisation rulebook discretionary powers as our bank's regulatory anchor is the ECB and Nordic FSAs for CRR implementation; we follow EBA Q&As and supervisory expectations on corporate credit risk capital treatment, not the UK regime.

items.title: PS7/25 – Update to PS9/24 on the SME and infrastructure lending adjustments
items.summary: The PRA has published near-final rules on Pillar 2A lending adjustments for SME and infrastructure exposures, replacing the removal of Pillar 1 support factors under Basel 3.1 implementation. The adjustments are firm-specific, calculated to hold overall capital requirements for SME and infrastructure lending constant, and will apply to exposures both before and after the Basel 3.1 implementation date. Firms must submit specified data templates to the PRA for calculation, with methodology detailed in the appended Statement of Policy.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must prepare data submissions and adjust capital models to reflect firm-specific Pillar 2A lending adjustments that offset the removal of Basel 3.1 SME and infrastructure support factors from Pillar 1.

quant.verdict: This is material to our IRB framework and capital models – I need to understand the PRA's firm-specific adjustment methodology, ensure our SME and infrastructure exposure data is correctly templated for submission, and cascade the Pillar 2A add-ons into our capital adequacy reporting and model governance before Basel 3.1 cutover.

regulatory.verdict: This PRA rule on SME and infrastructure Pillar 2A adjustments applies to UK banks under PRA supervision; as a Scandinavian bank, I am subject to ECB/EBA frameworks and Nordic FSA requirements where equivalent Basel 3.1 SME/infrastructure treatment will follow different EU regulatory architecture and timelines.

items.title: PS2/26 – Retiring the refined methodology to Pillar 2A – final
items.summary: The PRA has published final policy to retire the refined methodology to Pillar 2A, effective 1 January 2027, aligned with Basel 3.1 implementation. All PRA-regulated firms, including Small Domestic Deposit Takers, will transition to the Basel 3.1 standardised approach to credit risk on this date. This builds on the near-final policy published in PS18/25 with no material changes between near-final and final versions.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must update their capital adequacy frameworks to remove refined Pillar 2A methodologies and fully adopt Basel 3.1 standardised credit risk approaches by 1 January 2027.

quant.verdict: This is a capital framework transition that affects our overall regulatory capital position but does not directly impact PD/LGD model development, calibration, or validation since we are IRB-approach firms with standardised Pillar 2A charges—the refined methodology retirement is a Pillar 2A policy note rather than an IRB modelling mandate.

regulatory.verdict: I note this PRA policy on retiring refined Pillar 2A methodologies effective 1 January 2027, but as a Nordic bank with corporate exposures, this has limited direct impact on my CRR3/Basel 3.1 implementation roadmap since the PRA framework applies to UK-regulated entities, not my ECB and Nordic FSA supervisory perimeter—though I should monitor if EU/EEA coordination mirrors these timelines for consistency.

items.title: Regulatory Blog: Booking arrangements of third country branches in the EU – EBA publishes final RTS complementing the ongoing work on EU market access by non-EU banks
items.summary: The EBA has published final RTS on booking arrangements for third-country branches (TCBs) under CRD6, requiring TCBs to maintain comprehensive registries of all booked or originated assets, liabilities, and off-balance sheet items. The rules apply from January 11, 2027, and require TCBs to record positions with standardized information on counterparties, instruments, collateral, and risks. This booking requirement forms the basis for TCB classification and supervisory intensity under the harmonized CRD6 framework.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners at third-country branches must plan system and process investments to comply with new mandatory booking and registry requirements that will determine their regulatory classification and capital/liquidity treatment.

quant.verdict: While operationally important for TCB compliance teams, this RTS on booking arrangements has no direct bearing on my PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or IRB framework—it concerns booking mechanics and supervisory classification rather than modelling inputs or data quality standards that would affect model outcomes.

regulatory.verdict: This is relevant to EU branches of non-EU banks, but our Scandinavian bank is EU-domiciled; we do not operate third-country branches in the EU requiring these booking arrangements under CRD6, so the compliance burden and system implications do not apply to our corporate loan book operations.

items.title: EBF urges European Commission to simplify banking regulatory framework in new letter
items.summary: The EBF has submitted a letter to the European Commission urging simplification of the EU banking regulatory framework, citing excessive complexity from gold-plating, supervisory discretion, and overlapping requirements. The letter identifies capital requirements, macroprudential tools, and administrative burdens as key areas needing reform to improve competitiveness and financial stability.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners should monitor this advocacy position as it may signal upcoming regulatory relief or reform discussions that could affect capital requirement calculations, supervisory expectations, and compliance frameworks.

quant.verdict: This is advocacy positioning with no concrete regulatory change or modeling implication yet; I'll monitor if it crystallizes into actual framework amendments, but generic calls for simplification don't affect our current PD/LGD calibration, validation, or margin-of-conservatism decisions.

regulatory.verdict: While I note the EBF's advocacy for regulatory simplification, this is a lobbying position without concrete regulatory change, timeline, or binding supervisory mandate; I will monitor for follow-up from the Commission or specific CRR3 amendments, but current letter carries no direct operational impact on our capital treatment or reporting obligations.

items.title: PS9/24 – Implementation of the Basel 3.1 standards near-final part 2
items.summary: PRA publishes near-final policy statement PS9/24 implementing Basel 3.1 standards part 2, covering credit risk standardised approach, IRB approach, credit risk mitigation, output floor, and Pillar 3 disclosure and reporting. The document provides the PRA's feedback to consultation responses and near-final rules across multiple credit risk domains. Implementation awaits HM Treasury revocation of relevant CRR provisions before final rules can be issued.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must track these near-final rules for imminent changes to RWA measurement, capital ratio calculations, and reporting requirements across standardised and IRB approaches, with final implementation timeline dependent on HMT commencement regulations.

quant.verdict: PS9/24 Part 2 is directly material to my IRB model framework—the near-final rules on IRB approach, credit risk mitigation, and output floor will reshape our PD/LGD calibration constraints, capital floor mechanics, and supervisory validation expectations; I must integrate these requirements into our TRIM-ready model documentation and backtesting infrastructure immediately.

regulatory.verdict: This PRA policy statement on Basel 3.1 part 2 is UK-focused and has no direct bearing on my Nordic bank's capital treatment since we are regulated by ECB/Nordic FSAs under CRR3, not the PRA framework.

items.title: CP17/25 – Basel 3.1: Adjustments to the market risk framework
items.summary: PRA consultation paper on UK implementation of Basel 3.1 market risk framework amendments (FRTB), previously scheduled for 2026 and delayed to 1 January 2027. The framework introduces stricter trading book delineation, a recalibrated simplified standardised approach (SSA) for limited derivatives firms, a new advanced standardised approach (ASA) for broader users, and a new internal model approach (FRTB-IMA). EU has simultaneously delayed FRTB implementation by one year to 1 January 2027, citing international implementation uncertainty.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must track the delayed 2027 UK go-live for FRTB and related changes to standardised and internal model approaches for trading book risk capital calculations.

quant.verdict: This is a market risk framework update with no direct implication for my PD/LGD model development, calibration, or IRB validation work on corporate credit exposures.

regulatory.verdict: I monitor this for consistency between UK and EU timelines, but as a corporate credit risk specialist in a Nordic bank, FRTB's trading book delineation and market risk models have minimal impact on my day-to-day capital treatment of corporate loans in the banking book.

items.title: SS4/24 – Credit risk internal ratings based approach
items.summary: The PRA published SS4/24 on 12 September 2024, setting out supervisory expectations for IRB approach application in credit risk RWA calculation across 21 chapters covering permission, rating systems, PD/LGD/EAD model development and calibration, data representativeness, conservatism margins, and validation. The statement applies to PRA-authorised banks, building societies, investment firms, and holding companies.
items.why_it_matters: This is the primary PRA supervisory standard on IRB governance and technical implementation; IRB practitioners must ensure their rating systems, model calibrations, and validation practices align with these updated expectations.

quant.verdict: SS4/24 is foundational regulatory guidance that directly dictates how I develop, calibrate, and validate PD/LGD models; non-compliance on data representativeness, conservatism margins, or calibration methodology creates immediate model governance risk and supervisory action exposure, so I must ensure our entire framework is aligned with its 21 chapters before any model refresh cycle.

regulatory.verdict: This is a PRA statement targeted at UK-regulated entities; as a Nordic bank, our IRB governance and technical standards are set by the ECB, EBA, and Nordic FSAs under CRR3/CRD6, not PRA supervisory standards, so while IRB best practice may overlap, SS4/24 is not a direct regulatory obligation for our corporate exposures.

items.title: SS3/24 – Credit risk definition of default
items.summary: The PRA published SS3/24 on 12 September 2024, setting out supervisory expectations for the application of the definition of default under UK credit risk regulation. The statement covers past due criteria, unlikeliness-to-pay indicators, treatment of external data, return to non-defaulted status, consistency across portfolios, retail exposures, and documentation/governance requirements for PRA-authorised firms.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must align their default identification policies, processes, and governance with the PRA's expectations outlined in this supervisory statement, affecting IRB model calibration, provisioning assessments, and regulatory reporting.

quant.verdict: I must immediately integrate PRA SS3/24 into our default definition framework—this directly shapes IRB calibration (PD estimates hinge on accurate default event timing and consistency), influences downturn adjustments, and creates audit risk if our policies drift from supervisory expectations on past-due triggers, UTP indicators, or cure procedures.

regulatory.verdict: This PRA supervisory statement on UK credit risk definition of default is not applicable to my bank's Nordic corporate loan book, as the PRA regulates UK-domiciled firms and we operate under ECB/Nordics regulatory framework.

items.title: PRA to streamline supervision as part of 2026 priorities
items.summary: The UK PRA has published its 2026 supervisory priorities and announced a shift from annual to biennial Periodic Summary Meetings (PSMs) for larger firms starting 1 March 2026, coupled with streamlined timelines for senior manager applications, new firm authorisations, and IRB model change pre-approvals. The PRA also references continuation of Basel 3.1 implementation for larger firms and the "Strong and Simple" framework for smaller firms, alongside ongoing modernisation of reporting through the Future Banking Data project.
items.why_it_matters: The two-year PSM cycle and accelerated IRB pre-approval timelines change the cadence and pace of supervisory engagement on credit risk models and capital requirements, affecting planning and resourcing for practitioners.

quant.verdict: The biennial PSM cycle and streamlined IRB pre-approval timelines directly affect our model validation planning and supervisory interaction cadence; we need to front-load documentation and governance preparations to compress approval windows and adjust our annual cycle management accordingly.

regulatory.verdict: This is a UK PRA initiative affecting supervision of UK-domiciled banks; as a Nordic bank regulator by the ECB and Nordic FSAs, our supervisory engagement model, capital framework (CRR3/CRD6), and IRB governance are shaped by different authorities and timelines, so the PRA's shift to biennial PSMs and streamlined UK processes has no bearing on our corporate loan book capital treatment or compliance obligations.

items.title: Regulatory Blog: EBA Releases the Final Technical Package for ITS 4.2
items.summary: EBA released the final ITS 4.2 technical package on 25 November 2025, completing the migration to DPM 2.0 semantic reporting architecture for all major modules (COREP, FINREP, resolution, benchmarking, operational risk) except DORA. From reference dates around end-2025, institutions must report exclusively under this new framework with mandatory xBRL-CSV submission from 31 March 2026 onwards. The transition requires institutions to align internal data models to semantic regulatory concepts, implement enhanced validation controls, and prepare for increased supervisory scrutiny on cross-report consistency.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must understand that ITS 4.2 fundamentally changes COREP and supervisory reporting infrastructure; COREP-related credit risk data (exposures, capital, large exposures, counterparty risk) now flows through DPM 2.0 semantic definitions with stricter validation and cross-report consistency checks, requiring alignment of internal credit risk data governance and reconciliation procedures.

quant.verdict: ITS 4.2 and DPM 2.0 migration affects our COREP data governance and reconciliation procedures, but has no direct impact on PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or supervisory model review (TRIM) requirements—I need to ensure our credit risk data warehouse can feed regulatory reporting without model contamination.

regulatory.verdict: I must act now to ensure our credit risk data governance and COREP reporting infrastructure align with DPM 2.0 semantic definitions by end-2025, as this fundamentally reshapes how we report corporate credit exposures, capital treatment, and large exposures to supervisors.

items.title: Episode 53: The IOSCO Agenda
items.summary: ISDA has responded to the Reserve Bank of India's consultation on draft amendments to implement the standardized approach for counterparty credit risk (SA-CCR). ISDA broadly welcomes the RBI's move to SA-CCR and updated capital treatment for exposures, aligning with international regulatory standards.
items.why_it_matters: Regulators are progressing SA-CCR implementation globally; credit risk professionals must monitor jurisdictional divergences and supervisory expectations as emerging-market regulators adopt these standards.

quant.verdict: SA-CCR governs counterparty credit risk capital treatment for derivatives; as a corporate PD/LGD modeller, this sits outside my core IRB framework and has no direct implication for my model development, calibration, or validation work.

regulatory.verdict: While SA-CCR harmonization is systemically important, this item focuses on RBI implementation for derivative counterparty risk in India, which has minimal bearing on our Nordic corporate loan book and CRR3 timeline priorities.

items.title: ISDA In Review – December 2025
items.summary: ISDA published a response to the Reserve Bank of India's consultation on implementing the standardized approach for counterparty credit risk (SA-CCR). ISDA broadly welcomes the RBI's move to SA-CCR and updated capital treatment for exposures. This guidance aligns OTC derivatives regulation in India with international counterparty credit risk standards.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must track global adoption of SA-CCR as it affects capital calculations and risk management for counterparty exposures in jurisdictions where their firms operate.

quant.verdict: This is a capital and counterparty credit risk matter relevant to derivatives trading desks and treasury risk management, not to PD/LGD model development or IRB corporate credit risk calibration.

regulatory.verdict: I appreciate that SA-CCR harmonization is generally positive for international banking, but India's adoption of standardized counterparty credit risk approaches does not affect my corporate loan book capital treatment or regulatory reporting obligations in Nordic jurisdictions.

items.title: ISDA Responds to EC Targeted Consultation on Market Risk Prudential Framework
items.summary:
ISDA, AFME, and IIF submitted a joint response to the EC's targeted consultation on the market risk prudential framework, addressing the implementation of the Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB). The response reflects divided industry sentiment: 57% of respondents (by market risk RWA) support delaying FRTB implementation to ensure global consistency, while 43% favor the January 1, 2027 deadline. The associations propose bank-specific multipliers rather than industry-wide solutions to address capital neutrality concerns for firms negatively impacted by FRTB.</summary>
<parameter name="why_it_matters">Credit risk practitioners must track the EC's final stance on FRTB implementation timing and capital neutrality mechanisms, as these will directly affect trading book risk weighting, RWA calculations, and capital requirements for EU banks.

quant.verdict: While the FRTB framework affects RWA calculations bank-wide, this is a trading book market risk initiative that sits outside my PD/LGD model scope; I monitor the EC's final stance only for any spillover effects on corporate IRB parameters, downturn adjustments, or supervisory expectations around model conservatism, but the core technical content here does not impact my day-to-day PD/LGD model development or calibration work.

regulatory.verdict: While FRTB will affect our overall EU RWA and capital requirements, I'm a corporate credit risk specialist—this consultation addresses trading book and market risk frameworks that sit outside my remit; I'll monitor the EC's final timeline but this is not actionable for my corporate loan book work.

items.title: Updated OTC Derivatives Compliance Calendar
items.summary: ISDA has published an updated global compliance calendar for OTC derivatives regulatory deadlines and dates. The calendar covers over-the-counter derivatives regulatory obligations across jurisdictions. This is a reference tool for tracking compliance timelines in the derivatives space.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to monitor counterparty credit risk capital requirements (SA-CCR) implementation timelines and derivative-related regulatory compliance deadlines that may affect capital calculations and exposures.

quant.verdict: This is useful for derivatives operations and counterparty management, but for PD/LGD modelling of corporate exposures under IRB, it has minimal direct application—SA-CCR affects capital treatment not probability of default or loss given default estimation.

regulatory.verdict: While SA-CCR is relevant to counterparty risk, an ISDA global OTC derivatives calendar is too broad for my Nordic corporate loan book focus—my capital concerns are primarily on corporate credit risk under CRR3, not derivatives trading compliance across all jurisdictions.

items.title: ECB launches fast-track assessments for capital and securitisation
items.summary: The ECB has introduced two fast-track assessment processes effective January 2026 for standardised capital operations and significant risk transfer (SRT) securitisations, reducing approval timelines from three months to two weeks. Fast-track capital approvals apply to share buybacks and own funds reductions meeting specified criteria (capital impact <100 bps, medium/low-risk scoring, stress test adequacy). SRT securitisation fast-track requires standardised portfolios, capital impact <25 bps, and standardised contractual terms, with enhanced supervisory oversight on leveraged loans, concentration, and rollover risk.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners managing capital operations and securitisation programmes now have clearer, faster approval paths under standardised conditions, but must ensure strict compliance with fast-track eligibility criteria and heightened ECB scrutiny on leverage and leverage-loan concentration in securitised portfolios.

quant.verdict: This is a capital and securitisation workflow acceleration measure with no direct impact on PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or downturn estimation—important for treasury and capital planning teams, but outside the quantitative modelling remit.

regulatory.verdict: This is directly relevant to my capital management function—the fast-track regime effective January 2026 materially accelerates ECB approval for both own funds operations and SRT securitisations, but our bank must ensure Nordic corporate exposures in securitised portfolios meet the tighter leverage and concentration thresholds, particularly given ECB's signalled enhanced scrutiny on leveraged-loan concentration.

items.title: From Milestone to Modernization
items.summary: ISDA CEO outlines four modernization priorities for derivatives markets in 2026: completion of Basel III capital framework (including US regulatory revisions and EU trading book consultation); US Treasury clearing mandate implementation with focus on portfolio margining and CCR standardised approach netting recognition; harmonisation of fragmented derivatives data reporting rules via ESMA and FCA consultations; and use of tokenization to modernize collateral management and expand variation margin assets.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must track Basel III finalisation, counterparty credit risk netting treatment under SA-CCR for cleared products, and regulatory developments on portfolio margining that directly affect capital requirements and clearing economics.

quant.verdict: This is directly material to my PD/LGD modelling mandate: Basel III finalisation affects IRB capital floor calculations and downturn calibration constraints; SA-CCR netting recognition impacts counterparty credit risk treatment and potential changes to our exposure-at-default methodology; and portfolio margining shifts could alter collateral effectiveness assumptions in LGD. I need to monitor the US regulatory revisions and EU trading book consultation outputs closely for any changes to our model parameters or validation requirements.

regulatory.verdict: I need to track Basel III endgame capital framework finalisation, especially EU CRR3 trading book rules and US implementation timelines, as these directly impact how we treat derivative counterparty exposures and netting under SA-CCR for our corporate derivative portfolios.

items.title: ECB publishes supervisory banking statistics on significant institutions for the third quarter of 2025
items.summary: ECB publishes Q3 2025 supervisory banking statistics for significant institutions: aggregate CET1 ratio at 16.10%, non-performing loans ratio at 2.22%, and liquidity coverage ratio at 156.73%. The data shows capital ratios remain stable, NPL trends remain manageable with stage 2 loans decreasing, and liquidity metrics show a slight downward trend. Key country-level variations exist, with CET1 ratios ranging from 13.28% (Spain) to 23.12% (Lithuania).
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need this baseline data on capital adequacy, asset quality, and provisioning trends to benchmark internal portfolios and assess supervisory expectations for significant institutions under ECB direct supervision.

quant.verdict: Useful for benchmarking and supervisory context, but this aggregate macro data has limited direct impact on our IRB model architecture, PD/LGD calibration, or validation methodology—we build from borrower-level exposure data, not supervisory aggregates.

regulatory.verdict: I find this useful for macro-level benchmarking against our peer institutions and for understanding ECB supervisory expectations, but it provides limited direct guidance on our own capital treatment or Nordic-specific regulatory requirements; I will review the country-level breakdown to assess Scandinavian peer positioning.

items.title: AFME Urges Parliament to Take More Ambitious Approach on Securitisation
items.summary: AFME commentary on the EU Council's General Approach to the securitisation review, which amends the Securitisation Regulation and CRR. The Council position addresses delegation and sanctions concerns but introduces tightening of prudential requirements including €15bn UFCP thresholds, reporting burdens, UCITS caps, and risk weight floors that AFME argues lack sufficient risk sensitivity for mortgages, infrastructure, and project finance.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must track these securitisation framework changes affecting risk weights, UFCP eligibility, and capital treatment of structured credit transfers as Parliament develops its position in coming months.

quant.verdict: I need to monitor Parliament's securitisation review position since risk weight floors and UFCP thresholds will directly affect capital requirements and hedging strategies for structured credit, but the immediate modelling impact is secondary—we use these as constraints, not inputs to PD/LGD frameworks.

regulatory.verdict: I need to monitor AFME's securitisation lobbying closely because the Council's General Approach directly impacts our capital treatment of structured credit transfers and UFCP thresholds—Parliament's position will determine final CRR amendments affecting our corporate loan book securitisations and syndication strategies.

items.title: CP21/25 – Future banking data review: Deletion of banking reporting templates
items.summary: The PRA consultation paper (CP21/25) proposes deletion of certain banking regulatory reporting templates, initially focusing on Financial Reporting (FINREP) templates as part of its Future Banking Data programme. The initial phase is expected to reduce annual reporting burden for banks by approximately £26 million. Further workstreams involving partial deletions and reconfigurations of other data collections are planned for future phases.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to track which regulatory reporting templates are being eliminated or reconfigured, as this directly affects the scope and timing of their reporting obligations and internal data governance frameworks.

quant.verdict: I need to track the specifics of which FINREP and other templates are deleted, but absent a material change to the underlying data collection for IRB model inputs—particularly PD/LGD calibration datasets, downturn identification, or supervisory reporting of model parameters—this is primarily a regulatory reporting burden issue rather than a modelling governance issue.

regulatory.verdict: While I appreciate the PRA's push to streamline reporting, this UK-focused consultation on FINREP template deletions has limited direct relevance to my Nordic corporate loan book—my reporting obligations flow primarily through EBA-mandated frameworks and Nordic FSA regimes, not PRA dictates.

items.title: ECB to assess banks’ stress testing capabilities to capture geopolitical risk
items.summary: The ECB will conduct a reverse stress test on 110 directly supervised banks in 2026 to assess their stress-testing capabilities in capturing geopolitical risk. Banks must define scenarios leading to at least 300 basis point CET1 depletion and assess impacts across credit, market, liquidity, and operational risk categories. The exercise will be integrated into the 2026 ICAAP process, with aggregate results communicated in summer 2026, and findings will inform the supervisory review and evaluation process (SREP) qualitatively.
items.why_it_matters: Banks must now integrate geopolitical risk scenarios into their 2026 stress-testing frameworks and ICAAP submissions, with supervisory assessment of reverse stress-testing capabilities feeding into SREP evaluation and potentially affecting capital adequacy planning.

quant.verdict: This reverse stress test will directly affect how we specify credit risk (PD/LGD) scenarios, validate model responsiveness to geopolitical shocks, and demonstrate margin of conservatism in our downturn calibrations—findings will likely flow into SREP expectations on capital adequacy.

regulatory.verdict: I need to integrate geopolitical risk reverse stress-testing into our 2026 ICAAP framework and ensure our modelling captures cross-risk transmission mechanisms; ECB findings will directly influence our SREP outcome and capital planning.

items.title: EBF welcomes ECB report on banking rules simplification
items.summary: The European Banking Federation welcomes an ECB Governing Council report on simplification of EU banking rules, which proposes reducing capital stack complexity, streamlining the macroprudential framework, adopting risk-based supervision, and harmonising reporting requirements. The EBF supports these measures but calls for more ambitious action to eliminate overlapping requirements and address capital inefficiencies, citing a recent study showing supervisory requirements blocked EUR 1.5 trillion in lending capacity over three years.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must monitor these simplification proposals as they could reshape capital requirement frameworks, macroprudential add-ons, supervisory methodology, and reporting burden under EU/CRR3 implementation.

quant.verdict: I need to monitor the ECB's simplification proposals and any resulting CRR3/CRD6 amendments, but this remains a policy-level item with uncertain technical impact on our current PD/LGD models until concrete regulatory text emerges.

regulatory.verdict: I need to track ECB simplification proposals closely as they directly affect CRR3 implementation trajectory, potential relief in macroprudential frameworks, and supervisory expectations for our Nordic corporate portfolio—these could reshape capital efficiency and reporting obligations for years to come.

items.title: Regulatory Blog: Catalyzing Transparency: EBA Workshop Prepares Institutions for Pillar 3 Data Hub Launch
items.summary: The EBA held a comprehensive workshop on 25 November 2025 to support institutions' implementation of the Pillar 3 Data Hub (P3DH), a centralised platform for publishing CRR Article 434 disclosure reports. The EBA ITS/2025/01 (February 2025) sets detailed submission requirements for large and other institutions: quantitative data in XBRL-csv with explanatory notes, qualitative data in machine-readable PDF; onboarding for large institutions (Groups A–B) completed June–September 2025, with other institutions (Groups C–D) onboarded October–December 2025; 99% of 814 institutions were included, 58.1% submitted without errors. The P3DH go-live is imminent, with draft ITS on resubmissions to be developed in 2026.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must prepare technical systems to generate compliant XBRL-csv and PDF submissions meeting P3DH validation rules (DPM 4.1) by the go-live date and understand resubmission procedures and timeline.

quant.verdict: This is a Pillar 3 disclosure/reporting plumbing issue with no direct bearing on my PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or data architecture for IRB—it affects compliance and operational disclosure systems, not model assumptions or margin of conservatism.

regulatory.verdict: I must ensure our bank's technical infrastructure is ready to produce compliant XBRL-csv and PDF submissions for the P3DH go-live, and I need to track the 2026 ITS on resubmission procedures to understand our remediation timelines and operational obligations.

items.title: Governing Council proposes simplification of EU banking rules
items.summary: The ECB's Governing Council has published recommendations to simplify EU banking rules, including reducing elements in risk-weighted and leverage ratio frameworks, introducing a simpler regime for smaller banks, and merging capital buffers. The proposals aim to maintain banking system resilience while increasing proportionality, harmonising macroprudential rules, simplifying resolution requirements, and consolidating reporting across statistical, prudential and resolution purposes. The recommendations will be presented to the European Commission and inform the 2026 report on the banking system's overall situation.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must track these proposals as they could reshape capital requirement calculations, leverage ratios, IRB/standardised approach proportionality, and reporting obligations under CRR3 and future rules.

quant.verdict: These ECB proposals will materially affect our IRB model framework design, calibration scope, and regulatory approval requirements—I need to monitor the 2026 CRR3 trajectory closely to anticipate changes to PD/LGD validation perimeters and potential shifts between IRB and standardised approaches that could reshape our model portfolio.

regulatory.verdict: I need to monitor these ECB Governing Council recommendations closely as they directly signal what capital framework changes and proportionality adjustments may be coming in CRR3 revisions and beyond—this could materially affect how we calculate RWAs for Nordic corporates and our reporting footprint.

items.title: AFME UK Capital Markets Report Shows UK Maintains Global Financial Centre Status, But Loses Ground in IPOs, Green Finance and Global Equity
items.summary: AFME publishes UK Capital Markets Report benchmarking UK performance against global peers, highlighting declines in IPO activity, equity market share, and securitisation volumes. The report notes UK securitisation market true-sale activity averages only 1.6% of outstanding loans versus 6.8% in the US, and identifies challenges in household investment patterns and ESG bond issuance.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to track securitisation market depth and activity as it affects loan-to-capital-market conversion capacity, regulatory capital requirements, and counterparty exposures under CRR securitisation and credit risk mitigation frameworks.

quant.verdict: While securitisation market health is genuinely important for capital planning and CRM frameworks, this report is a high-level competitive benchmarking exercise that doesn't provide the granular origination, counterparty, or methodological data I need to validate assumptions in our PD/LGD models or adjust our downturn scenarios.

regulatory.verdict: While securitisation market depth is relevant to capital frameworks, this UK-centric benchmark report has no material bearing on my Nordic corporate loan book, CRR3 implementation, or supervisory expectations from ECB/Nordic FSAs.

items.title: AFME Welcomes ECB Ambitions to Simplify EU Banking Rules
items.summary: AFME welcomes the ECB's High Level Task Force report identifying 17 areas for simplification in EU banking regulation but calls for concrete follow-up action. The statement covers multiple credit risk topics including capital buffer complexity, MREL framework simplification, cross-border regulatory barriers affecting capital and liquidity allocation, and supervisory intensity concerns. AFME emphasizes that simplification should not increase capital costs and that wider regulatory fragmentation issues remain unaddressed.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners should monitor the ECB's simplification agenda and potential changes to capital requirements, MREL, buffer frameworks, and supervisory expectations that could affect capital planning and compliance costs.

quant.verdict: This is regulatory advocacy commentary with no direct implication for PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or data requirements—it concerns capital framework architecture and supervisory policy, not the underlying credit risk quantification work.

regulatory.verdict: I'll monitor the ECB's simplification taskforce output closely, particularly around capital buffer frameworks and MREL mechanics that could reshape our Nordic corporate capital treatment, but AFME advocacy statements alone carry limited weight without concrete ECB follow-through or FSA guidance.

items.title: Machine-readable Pillar 3 disclosure
items.summary: The Basel Committee on Banking Supervision has issued a consultation proposing that banks make Pillar 3 disclosure data available in standardised machine-readable formats rather than PDF-only. The proposal sets out technical specifications for machine-readable quantitative disclosures without changing underlying disclosure requirements. National supervisors will determine whether banks publish these disclosures on their own websites or via a centralised repository.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners will need to adapt their reporting processes and systems to comply with new machine-readable disclosure format requirements once finalised.

quant.verdict: This will create operational burden for reporting infrastructure but has no direct impact on model development, validation, or calibration—I care about it only insofar as it affects the consistency and accessibility of historical disclosure data we might use for backtesting or supervisory reporting validation.

regulatory.verdict: I need to monitor this Basel consultation closely for the EBA's and Nordic FSAs' implementation approach—machine-readable Pillar 3 will eventually affect our regulatory reporting infrastructure and internal systems, but won't impact capital treatment of our corporate exposures directly.

items.title: Steps to a Vibrant and Resilient Derivatives Market: Scott O’Malia Remarks
items.summary: ISDA CEO Scott O'Malia discusses ongoing Basel III implementation including the Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB) across major jurisdictions, noting that the EU and UK are scheduled to implement FRTB from January 2027. He emphasizes the importance of risk-sensitive capital requirements, consistency across borders, and highlights current fragmentation in timing and content of rules between the US, EU, and UK. ISDA is advocating for targeted changes to capital requirements to ensure they appropriately reflect underlying risks and maintain market resilience.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to track Basel III capital framework alignment across jurisdictions and timing, as fragmentation between US, EU, and UK implementations will affect how global banks manage counterparty credit risk and capital planning.

quant.verdict: FRTB is a trading book framework; my models cover corporate credit risk under the IRB approach for non-trading exposures, so this derivatives market discussion has no direct bearing on PD/LGD calibration, data requirements, or credit risk model validation in my mandate.

regulatory.verdict: I need to monitor EU/UK FRTB implementation timelines from January 2027, but this derivatives market perspective has limited direct bearing on my corporate loan book capital treatment—our trading book and counterparty credit risk teams own this space more than corporate risk management does.

items.title: 90% of banks’ retained earnings tied up in meeting increasing supervisory capital requirements from 2021 to 2024
items.summary: The European Banking Federation study found that between 2021 and 2024, supervisory discretionary CET1 capital requirements increased by EUR 102 billion beyond Basel baseline requirements across 15 major European banks. The study shows that 90% of the EUR 112 billion in retained earnings generated by these banks was absorbed by this increase, limiting lending capacity expansion. The EBF calls for policymakers to reduce "gold-plating" of capital requirements beyond Basel minimums to improve credit availability to the economy.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners face evolving supervisory capital expectations that significantly exceed Basel floors and constrain business strategy; this quantification of the supervisory add-on burden informs capital planning and advocacy positions.

quant.verdict: This is useful context for understanding the supervisory landscape and capital planning pressures, but it does not directly affect my PD/LGD modelling work or data requirements—the capital add-ons are driven by supervisory discretion and SREP outcomes, not by model development or calibration choices on my side.

regulatory.verdict: I need to understand the scope and trajectory of supervisory add-ons in Nordic jurisdictions specifically, as this EBF study quantifies the material drag on retained earnings that affects our capital planning and regulatory headroom for corporate lending growth—this is directly actionable intelligence for our three-year ICAAP cycle and ECB/FSA engagement strategy.

items.title: Regulators announce plans to support growth of mutuals sector
items.summary: UK regulators (PRA/FCA) announced a package of measures to support mutuals sector growth, including a review of credit union regulations considering risk-based requirements for larger firms and proportionality for smaller ones, removal of the Building Societies Sourcebook from the PRA rulebook, and establishment of a Mutual Societies Development Unit. The PRA notes these are part of initiatives to simplify capital requirements for smaller firms and support the wider financial sector.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners in UK mutuals (credit unions, building societies) face potential changes to capital and provisioning rules and regulatory proportionality that could affect their compliance burden and risk frameworks.

quant.verdict: While this touches on capital and provisioning rules for UK mutuals, it remains at the policy announcement level with no concrete changes to IRB model requirements, data standards, or validation frameworks—and our corporate PD/LGD work sits outside the mutual credit union and building society segment, so any proportionality relief or sourcebook removals have no direct bearing on our models or supervisory reviews.

regulatory.verdict: I monitor UK regulatory developments as a matter of professional awareness, but this mutuals sector initiative has no bearing on my Nordic corporate loan book—we focus on CRR3, EBA mandates, and ECB/Nordic FSA supervisory expectations for Scandinavian corporates.

items.title: CP11/25 – Discontinuing SS20/15: Supervising building societies’ treasury and lending activities
items.summary: The PRA is consulting on the deletion of Supervisory Statement SS20/15, which sets supervisory expectations for building societies on treasury and lending activities under the Building Societies Act 1986 and FSMA. The PRA concludes that SS20/15's prescriptive expectations are no longer consistent with its broader policy approach and create a level-playing field issue versus banks. Withdrawal of SS20/15 does not require rulebook changes and is expected to help building societies compete without imposing sector costs.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners supervising UK building societies must understand that prescriptive lending and treasury supervisory expectations tied to SS20/15 will no longer apply, shifting to principles-based oversight aligned with bank supervision and existing PRA credit risk tools.

quant.verdict: While this concerns credit risk supervision of building societies, the shift from SS20/15 to principles-based oversight does not materially impact PD/LGD model development, calibration, or validation methodologies—it affects supervisory expectations rather than model framework or data requirements.

regulatory.verdict: This UK PRA consultation on building societies' supervisory framework is immaterial to my Nordic corporate loan book and CRR3 capital framework responsibilities.

items.title: PS26/25 – Discontinuing SS20/15: Supervising building societies’ treasury and lending activities
items.summary: The PRA finalises withdrawal of Supervisory Statement 20/15 on building societies' treasury and lending activities, effective 5 December 2025, with consequential amendments to SS31/15 on ICAAP and SREP. The statement concludes that prescriptive expectations in SS20/15 are outdated, represent a competitive disadvantage for building societies relative to banks, and are no longer necessary given improved sector risk management and existing supervisory tools. Four of seven consultation respondents supported the withdrawal, while the PRA brought forward the implementation date from 1 January 2026 following feedback.
items.why_it_matters: Building societies can now manage treasury and lending activities without the prescriptive requirements of SS20/15, subject instead to general PRA supervisory expectations and existing standards including ICAAP/SREP—clarifying the regulatory framework for treasury and lending risk management in this sector.

quant.verdict: This is a building society treasury/lending supervisory statement withdrawal that does not impact corporate PD/LGD model development, calibration, or validation frameworks under IRB—it addresses prescriptive expectations for a non-bank sector outside our core modelling remit.

regulatory.verdict: This PRA update on building societies' treasury requirements has no bearing on my Nordic corporate loan book and capital treatment framework.

items.title: ECB concludes asset quality review of Raiffeisen-Holding Niederösterreich-Wien
items.summary: The ECB published results of its asset quality review (AQR) for Raiffeisen-Holding Niederösterreich-Wien, a bank recently classified as significant and placed under direct ECB supervision in January 2025. The AQR focused on credit risk across corporate and retail portfolios, finding no capital shortfall as the bank's CET1 ratio remains at 19.46% after adjustments (267 basis points impact). The bank's CET1 ratio stays above the 8% minimum threshold and supervisors will consider AQR results in the Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process (SREP).
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to understand ECB's supervisory expectations for asset valuation and capital adequacy, particularly the credit risk assessment methodology applied to significant institutions' corporate and retail portfolios under the SREP framework.

quant.verdict: The AQR outcome itself has limited direct impact on my PD/LGD modelling work—a specific bank's capital adequacy and portfolio assessments don't inform our model frameworks—but the supervisory methodology and expectations embedded in the ECB's credit risk assessment and SREP integration warrant monitoring for calibration rigour and potential margin of conservatism implications in future reviews.

regulatory.verdict: While this AQR demonstrates ECB's supervisory rigor, it concerns an Austrian bank's portfolio assessment and holds limited precedent for my Nordic corporate exposures; I would prioritize ECB guidance on cross-border Nordic bank groups or sector-wide AQR findings instead.

items.title: Regulatory Blog: Webinar: The Next Phase of IRB  – The new Age of IRB Models
items.summary: PwC webinar recording (November 2025) on machine learning applications in IRB credit risk models, covering ECB expectations per the July 2025 Guide to Internal Models, governance and validation requirements, and market developments in ML-based credit risk assessment across the lending lifecycle.
items.why_it_matters: IRB practitioners need to understand ECB's latest expectations on ML governance, validation, and explainability in internal models, and how market practice is evolving around hybrid traditional-ML approaches.

quant.verdict: I must review this webinar carefully—ECB's July 2025 Guide to Internal Models directly shapes our TRIM preparation, model governance framework, and validation playbook, especially around ML explainability and hybrid approaches that could affect how we defend our PD/LGD architecture to supervisors.

regulatory.verdict: I need to track ECB's July 2025 Guide to Internal Models and its specific expectations on ML governance and validation, as these will directly shape our IRB model framework and supervisory dialogue for corporate exposures.

items.title: Addressing Termination Troubles
items.summary: ISDA CEO discusses operational and legal challenges in terminating OTC derivatives contracts during counterparty distress, noting accelerated failure timelines (exemplified by SVB's 2-day collapse). The article covers ISDA's close-out framework, practical seminars for financial institutions, and the new Notices Hub platform to facilitate rapid termination notice delivery. Over 145 entities including 65% of global primary dealers have adopted the 2025 Notices Hub Protocol to reduce risks from notice delivery delays.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to understand operational playbooks and legal frameworks for counterparty close-outs, as regulatory stays on terminations and collateral segregation rules now complicate the process that must execute in days rather than weeks.

quant.verdict: This is relevant to our CCR framework and recovery assumptions, but doesn't directly inform PD/LGD model development—the legal and operational close-out infrastructure affects our loss-given-default scenarios and collateral availability in stress, which we should monitor through our counterparty risk team rather than embed into corporate credit models.

regulatory.verdict: While counterparty risk management is professionally relevant, this item addresses OTC derivatives operational infrastructure and ISDA protocols that do not materially affect capital treatment, CRR3 implementation, or supervisory mandates specific to my Nordic corporate loan book.

items.title: ISDA In Review – November 2025
items.summary: ISDA submitted a response to the Reserve Bank of India's consultation on draft amendment directions on the standardized approach for counterparty credit risk (SA-CCR). ISDA broadly welcomes the RBI's move to adopt SA-CCR and updated capital treatment for exposures, indicating industry support for this regulatory alignment.
items.why_it_matters: RBI's adoption of SA-CCR will affect capital calculations for counterparty credit risk in Indian banks; ISDA's feedback signals alignment with international standards relevant to cross-border derivatives exposure.

quant.verdict: This is a counterparty credit risk capital methodology matter for derivatives under SA-CCR, which sits outside the IRB scope that governs my PD/LGD model work; it has no direct bearing on my corporate exposure model development, calibration, or validation practices.

regulatory.verdict: While SA-CCR harmonization is conceptually valuable, RBI's Indian regulatory space does not affect our Nordic corporate loan book capital treatment or our CRR3 implementation timelines.

items.title: Regulatory Consistency Assessment Programme (RCAP): Assessment of Basel Committee's large exposures framework – the United Kingdom
items.summary: The Basel Committee's RCAP report assesses the UK's implementation of the Basel large exposures framework (LEX). The UK's LEX regulations have been found to be largely compliant with the Basel Framework, demonstrating consistency in how large exposures are regulated and calculated across jurisdictions.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners in UK-regulated institutions should confirm their large exposures calculations align with the Basel standards and UK rules, as this assessment validates regulatory consistency but may highlight any remaining areas needing remediation.

quant.verdict: Large exposures framework compliance is a risk management and regulatory reporting matter, not a PD/LGD model development or calibration concern for IRB.

regulatory.verdict: This UK-focused LEX assessment has no direct bearing on my Nordic corporate loan book or Scandinavian regulatory framework—our bank follows CRR/CRD requirements and Nordic FSA guidance, not UK PRA rules.

items.title: Regulatory Consistency Assessment Programme (RCAP) - Handbook for jurisdictional assessments
items.summary: The Basel Committee has published a revised Handbook for the Regulatory Consistency Assessment Programme (RCAP) that sets out methodology for assessing domestic prudential regulations against the Basel framework. This version includes specific guidance on assessing Basel III revisions to risk-weighted assets (RWA) and the leverage ratio framework. The handbook serves as a reference for jurisdictional compliance assessments conducted by the BCBS.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to understand the RCAP assessment methodology and Basel III RWA/leverage ratio standards that underpin supervisory evaluation of their jurisdiction's CRR implementation and capital adequacy frameworks.

quant.verdict: I need to understand RCAP assessment methodology and Basel III RWA standards for capital adequacy, but this handbook is primarily a supervisory assessment tool rather than a direct input to my PD/LGD model development or validation work.

regulatory.verdict: I need to monitor RCAP findings for consistency implications in our Nordic jurisdiction, but the handbook itself is primarily a Basel Committee assessment tool rather than a direct regulatory requirement we must implement.

items.title: PS20/25 – The Strong and Simple Framework: The simplified capital regime for Small Domestic Deposit Takers (SDDTs)
items.summary: The PRA publishes near-final policy PS20/25 establishing a simplified capital regime for Small Domestic Deposit Takers (SDDTs), including streamlined Pillar 1, Pillar 2A, buffers, and reporting requirements. The regime simplifies credit risk approaches (standardised approach) and will be finalised once HM Treasury implements Basel 3.1 commencement regulations. The simplified capital stack applies proportionate capital requirements while maintaining resilience for domestically focused deposit-taking firms.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners at SDDTs and their supervisors must implement significantly simplified IRB/standardised approaches, Pillar 2A methodologies, and reporting, with final rules to follow Basel 3.1 implementation by HM Treasury.

quant.verdict: PS20/25 is supervisory architecture for SDDTs that mandates standardised approach (not IRB), so it has no direct bearing on my PD/LGD model development, calibration, or validation work at a European bank operating under the full IRB framework.

regulatory.verdict: This PRA policy on simplified SDDT capital regimes is not relevant to my role—it applies to UK small domestic deposit takers under PRA supervision, whereas I work in a Scandinavian bank's corporate credit risk function where CRR3, EBA guidance, and Nordic FSA expectations are the governing framework.

items.title: Trading Book Capital: Basel III Implementation and Latest Industry Trends: Mark Gheerbrant Welcome Remarks
items.summary: ISDA's Mark Gheerbrant discusses 2026 priorities for Basel III implementation across major jurisdictions: the US is revising its endgame proposal, the EU launched a targeted FRTB consultation with temporary relief measures (closing January 6), and the UK will implement Basel 3.1 in 2027. ISDA advocates for risk-sensitive capital frameworks and highlights concerns about reduced viability of internal models under FRTB, with only 10 of 26 banks surveyed planning to use them for reduced trading desk scope.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to track Basel III endgame revisions in the US, EU FRTB consultation deadlines and temporary measures, UK implementation timelines, and shifting internal models adoption—all of which will materially change capital calculations and model requirements through 2026–2027.

quant.verdict: While Basel III implementation is strategically important to the bank, this trading book capital and FRTB discussion has minimal direct impact on my PD/LGD corporate IRB models—it concerns market risk capital frameworks and internal models for trading desks, not credit risk parameters or downturn estimation for corporate exposures.

regulatory.verdict: I track corporate CRR3 capital treatment and regulatory reporting for Nordic bank exposures, not trading book rules—FRTB and Basel III endgame are primarily asset-side concerns for the trading desk, and this item's focus on internal models viability and jurisdictional trading book implementation timelines sits outside my remit.

items.title: AFME welcomes FPC review of UK bank capital requirements
items.summary: AFME welcomes the UK Financial Policy Committee's (FPC) reassessment of UK bank capital requirements, the first comprehensive review in six years. The FPC has revised its assessment of the appropriate benchmark for system-wide Tier 1 capital requirements, reflecting decreased banking sector risks. Further review is planned on the leverage ratio framework and interactions between capital requirements to ensure buffers are useable and promote international alignment.
items.why_it_matters: UK capital requirement benchmarks and leverage ratio calibration are being revisited; practitioners should monitor the FPC and PRA's next steps on capital buffer usability and international alignment with Basel standards.

quant.verdict: This is a macro-prudential policy signal I should monitor for potential supervisory impacts on IRB model assumptions and capital floor calibration, but it doesn't directly affect my current PD/LGD model mechanics or data requirements unless the PRA translates this into changes to TRIM review expectations or regulatory capital floors that bind our models."

regulatory.verdict: This UK FPC review does not affect my Nordic corporate loan book or capital treatment framework, as it concerns UK domestic benchmarks outside CRR3/EBA scope and does not signal changes relevant to Nordic FSA expectations or Scandinavian banking regulation.

items.title: Updated OTC Derivatives Compliance Calendar
items.summary: ISDA has updated its global compliance calendar for OTC derivatives regulatory deadlines. The update references ISDA's response to the RBI consultation on standardized approach for counterparty credit risk (SA-CCR) implementation and updated capital treatment for exposures.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need clarity on SA-CCR implementation timelines and capital treatment changes, particularly if their institutions operate under RBI jurisdiction or reference RBI guidance.

quant.verdict: Not material to my PD/LGD model work; SA-CCR is a counterparty credit risk exposure measurement framework orthogonal to our corporate probability of default and loss given default calibration, and compliance calendar mechanics have no bearing on model development, validation, or downturn estimation.

regulatory.verdict: This is immaterial to my responsibilities—ISDA's RBI consultation on SA-CCR has no bearing on our Nordic corporate loan book or CRR3 implementation timelines under ECB/Nordic FSA supervision.

items.title: Other systemically important institutions (O-SII) buffer rates for ring-fenced banks, large domestic banks, and large building societies
items.summary: The PRA has set O-SII buffer rates for ring-fenced banks and large building societies, effective from 1 January 2027, based on the FPC's updated framework. Rates range from 1.0% to 2.0% of RWAs for RFBs (Lloyds 2.0%, NatWest 1.5%, HSBC/Barclays/Santander UK 1.0%) and 1.0% for Nationwide Building Society. Where O-SII buffers exceed G-SIB buffers, both the group PRA buffer and Leverage Ratio Group Add-on will increase accordingly.
items.why_it_matters: Capital requirement practitioners must update their 2027 capital planning and Pillar 2B calculations to reflect these new O-SII buffer rates, which will affect consolidated and sub-consolidated capital adequacy for major UK lenders.

quant.verdict: This is administratively important for capital planning but has no impact on my PD/LGD model development, calibration, or validation work—it's a capital requirement mechanics issue that our capital planning team owns.

regulatory.verdict: This UK PRA decision on O-SII buffers for ring-fenced banks and building societies does not affect my Scandinavian bank's Nordic corporate loan book or capital treatment under CRR3, as it concerns UK-specific regulatory buffers that do not apply to our regulatory perimeter.

items.title: 2025 list of UK headquartered Globally Systemically Important Institutions (G-SIIs)
items.summary: The PRA announced the 2025 list of UK-headquartered G-SIIs comprising HSBC Holdings Plc (sub-category 3, 389 score, 2% G-SII buffer), Barclays Plc (sub-category 2, 291 score, 1.5% buffer), and Standard Chartered Plc (sub-category 1, 142 score, 1% buffer). The G-SII buffer requirements will apply from 1 January 2027, with annual updates to follow.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners at G-SIIs and their counterparties must plan capital adequacy strategies around the now-confirmed G-SII buffer rates and their 1 January 2027 implementation date.

quant.verdict: This affects my capital framework planning and validates our baseline assumptions for counterparty modelling, but doesn't directly change PD/LGD methodology or data requirements for corporate exposures.

regulatory.verdict: This concerns UK G-SIIs under PRA jurisdiction; as a Nordic bank regulator's concern, it has minimal bearing on our Scandinavian corporate loan book or capital treatment framework which falls under CRR3 and ECB/Nordic FSA supervision.

items.title: 2025 list of UK firms designated as Other Systemically Important Institutions (O-SIIs)
items.summary: The PRA has published its annual identification of 13 UK firms designated as Other Systemically Important Institutions (O-SIIs) for 2025, including major banks such as Barclays, HSBC, Lloyds, and NatWest. O-SII designation triggers higher capital buffer requirements under the Capital Requirements Regulations. This list is determined through the PRA's established methodology and policy framework for systemic importance assessment.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners in designated O-SIIs must apply higher countercyclical and systemic risk buffers, increasing capital requirements on credit exposures and affecting RWA and capital planning.

quant.verdict: This confirms regulatory capital treatment for UK O-SIIs but has no direct bearing on PD/LGD model development, calibration or validation – it affects RWA mechanics downstream, not the credit risk parameters themselves.

regulatory.verdict: This is a UK PRA designation list with no direct bearing on my Nordic corporate loan book or CRR3/EBA frameworks that govern our bank's capital treatment.

items.title: EBF Board Highlights | 26 November 2025
items.summary: The European Banking Federation Board discusses regulatory simplification and competitiveness, highlighting that supervisory capital requirements across 15 major EU banks increased by €102 billion between 2021–2024 while the Basel baseline remained flat, absorbing 90% of retained earnings and blocking €1.5 trillion in potential lending. The Board emphasises the need for ambitious securitisation reform and calls for faster action to reduce the complexity of the EU regulatory and supervisory framework to match reforms in the US and UK.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to track the growing supervisory capital add-ons (€102 billion increase) and the push for securitisation framework reform, as these directly affect capital requirements, lending capacity, and the practical implementation of CRR/supervisory discretion in the EU.

quant.verdict: The supervisory capital add-on trend (€102B increase over 3 years) is a macro indicator of supervisory conservatism and discretion that affects our model validation baseline and ECB TRIM dialogue, but the aggregated observation doesn't directly translate to model changes unless it signals systematic tightening in how PD/LGD multipliers or downturn assumptions are being applied across the industry.

regulatory.verdict: I need to monitor EBF's securitisation reform advocacy and the €102 billion supervisory add-on trend closely, as these directly impact our capital treatment of corporates and the regulatory burden our bank will face under CRR3 implementation and ongoing ECB supervisory practices.

items.title: Doubling Down on Appropriate Trading Book Capital
items.summary:
ISDA CEO commentary on Basel III completion and the Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB) implementation across US, EU, and UK. Discusses divergence in jurisdictional approaches, risk of reduced internal model usage due to stringent approval processes, and the need for risk-sensitive capital calibration rather than temporary relief measures. ISDA recommends recalibration of profit-and-loss attribution tests, risk factor eligibility tests, and non-modellable risk factors to maintain incentives for internal models.</summary>
<parameter name="why_it_matters">Capital risk practitioners need to monitor divergent FRTB implementation timelines (EU/UK 2027, US pending revision) and prepare for a potential shift to standardized approaches as fewer banks qualify for internal models.

quant.verdict: This is a trading book capital framework discussion with no implications for our corporate PD/LGD models, IRB approach, or data governance in the banking book.

regulatory.verdict: While FRTB implementation is relevant to the bank's capital framework, this ISDA commentary addresses trading book treatment where our focus is corporate loan exposures in the banking book, and the divergent US/EU/UK policy recommendations don't materially affect our Nordic corporate CRR3 implementation priorities or supervisory reporting obligations.

items.title: Regulatory Blog: Video series Securitisation Unlocked: (5) NPE Management – time for real action?
items.summary: PwC's "Securitisation Unlocked" video series Part 5 addresses non-performing exposure (NPE) management strategies for European banks. The discussion covers rising NPE volumes, anticipated CET1 deductions from regulatory backstops, and management approaches including securitisation, portfolio sales, and credit fund solutions. The series highlights how Southern European banks have successfully implemented NPE strategies in response to evolving regulatory requirements.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to understand current NPE management frameworks and securitisation solutions as regulatory backstops increasingly drive CET1 deductions and reshape capital management strategies.

quant.verdict: While NPE management is operationally important, this is a general regulatory blog focused on capital management strategy and securitisation execution—not on PD/LGD model development, calibration, downturn estimation, or validation frameworks that shape my day-to-day modelling work.

regulatory.verdict: While NPE management and securitisation mechanics are operationally relevant, this PwC video blog offers general industry perspective rather than Nordic-specific supervisory guidance, regulatory mandate updates, or capital treatment changes that would materially affect my bank's corporate loan book strategy or compliance roadmap.

items.title: Reshaping the Global IRD Landscape: Key Findings from the 2025 BIS Triennial Survey
items.summary: BIS Triennial Survey reports OTC interest rate derivatives (IRD) global average daily turnover rose 60% to $7.9 trillion between April 2022 and April 2025, driven by strong euro-denominated growth. The UK remains the leading IRD trading center, while EU-based desks recorded marked increases as euro transactions migrated to continental Europe. US-based turnover grew more slowly, reducing its global market share.
items.why_it_matters: Understanding shifts in OTC IRD market structure and geographic concentration is essential for credit risk practitioners assessing counterparty exposure concentrations and implementing SA-CCR under CRR/Basel 3.

quant.verdict: While market structure shifts are operationally useful for treasury and counterparty credit risk teams, this OTC IRD turnover survey has no direct implication for my PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, downturn estimation, or supervisory model reviews.

regulatory.verdict: While this BIS survey documents important structural shifts in global IRD markets, it lacks direct relevance to my Nordic corporate loan book capital treatment and supervisory mandate work.

items.title: ECB keeps capital requirements broadly stable for 2026 amid persisting global challenge
items.summary: ECB published 2025 SREP results for 105 directly supervised banks, keeping overall CET1 capital requirements broadly stable at 11.2% for 2026 with Pillar 2 requirements at 1.2%. Qualitative supervisory measures focus primarily on credit risk (40%), governance, and capital adequacy, with fewer new measures issued than prior year. Non-binding Pillar 2 guidance decreased from 1.3% to 1.1% based on EU-wide stress test results showing higher losses but improved profit absorption.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must adjust compliance frameworks to the 2026 capital requirements and track intensified supervisory focus on NPE management, Stage 2 loans, and commercial real estate/SME portfolio credit risk assessments.

quant.verdict: This SREP outcome directly affects our PD/LGD model validation timeline and downturn estimation assumptions for 2026, particularly as ECB maintains 11.2% CET1 requirements while tightening scrutiny on NPE and Stage 2 classification—I need to ensure our credit risk segmentation (especially CRE and SME) reflects these supervisory expectations and stress test calibrations.

regulatory.verdict: I need to immediately cascade the 2026 SREP CET1 and Pillar 2 requirements into our capital planning models and stress test assumptions, given that credit risk supervisory focus at 40% directly targets our corporate loan book assessment practices—particularly NPE management, Stage 2 identification, and CRE/SME portfolio risk granularity.

items.title: Highlights of the Basel III monitoring exercise as of 31 December 2024
items.summary: The Basel Committee's semiannual monitoring exercise reports capital and liquidity metrics as of 31 December 2024 for 176 banks (116 Group 1, 59 Group 2). Under full Basel III phase-in, Group 1 Tier 1 MRC would increase by 1.4%, driven by output floor (+1.7%), market risk (+1.2%), and credit risk (+0.5%), with significant regional variation (Europe +2.5%, Americas +0.9%). Group 1 CET1 ratio rose to 14.0% from 13.4% in H1 2024, while leverage ratios remained stable and LCR decreased slightly.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners need to track the output floor and credit risk components driving the 1.4% increase in Tier 1 MRC, particularly for European banks facing +2.5% impact, which will shape capital management and stress-testing assumptions.

quant.verdict: I need to carefully track how the output floor (+1.7%) and credit risk (+0.5%) components are driving the Tier 1 MRC increase, especially the regional breakdown showing Europe at +2.5%, as this directly impacts our IRB model conservatism requirements, capital buffer assumptions in stress testing, and the margin of conservatism we must embed in PD/LGD calibrations to remain compliant under full phase-in.

regulatory.verdict: I need to closely monitor this Basel III monitoring data, especially the +2.5% Tier 1 MRC impact for European banks and the output floor dynamics, as these directly inform our Nordic bank's capital planning and will shape how ECB/FSA supervisory expectations evolve around credit risk treatment and the output floor calibration.

items.title: DP2/25 – Alternative Life Capital: Supporting innovation in the life insurance sector
items.summary: The PRA has issued a discussion paper on alternative capital structures for UK life insurers, exploring regulatory reforms to facilitate risk transfer to capital markets through mechanisms such as Insurance Special Purpose Vehicles (ISPVs) or approaches similar to banking's Significant Risk Transfers. The paper invites industry feedback on potential policy changes and supervisory frameworks to enable life insurers better access to alternative capital sources while maintaining policyholder protection. Responses are requested by 6 February 2026.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners in insurance must understand how proposed reforms to ISPV frameworks and risk transfer mechanisms could reshape capital adequacy requirements and solvency capital allocations for life insurers, with potential implications for insurance-linked securitisation and prudential regulation.

quant.verdict: This is a UK life insurance capital structure discussion paper with no direct implication for my corporate PD/LGD model development, validation, or IRB calibration work.

regulatory.verdict: I focus on capital treatment and supervisory expectations for Nordic corporate loan books under CRR3 and ECB/FSA mandates; UK PRA reforms to life insurer capital structures have no bearing on my responsibilities for corporate credit risk in Scandinavian banking.

items.title: PS22/25 – Leverage Ratio: Changes to the retail deposits threshold for application of the requirement
items.summary: The PRA finalised changes to the UK leverage ratio framework, increasing the retail deposits threshold for application from £50 billion to £75 billion and introducing a three-year averaging mechanism for calculating retail deposits. The rules apply to CRR firms and consolidation entities on an individual, consolidated, and sub-consolidated basis. The policy takes effect on 1 January 2026.
items.why_it_matters: Firms need to reassess their leverage ratio compliance scope using the new £75 billion threshold and three-year averaging metric to determine when they become subject to the requirement.

quant.verdict: This is a leverage ratio threshold adjustment with no direct implications for PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or downturn estimation—it affects capital ratio compliance mechanics rather than credit risk parameters.

regulatory.verdict: This PRA rule on UK leverage ratio thresholds does not apply to our Nordic corporate exposures or capital framework—we operate under CRR3 and Nordic FSA rules, not UK prudential regime post-Brexit.

items.title: ISDA SIMM: The Trusted Standard for Initial Margin Calculations
items.summary: The ISDA Standard Initial Margin Model (ISDA SIMM) is a widely-adopted risk-sensitive framework for calculating initial margin on non-cleared derivatives, calibrated to cover portfolio value changes over 10 days at 99% confidence. The model is subject to global regulatory review, uses standard Greek-based sensitivities, and undergoes independent validation every three years. Standardized use of ISDA SIMM across the industry reduces margin disputes and mitigates counterparty credit risk.
items.why_it_matters: Credit risk practitioners must understand how initial margin calculations under ISDA SIMM affect counterparty credit risk mitigation and regulatory capital requirements for non-cleared derivatives exposures.

quant.verdict: While ISDA SIMM is operationally important for counterparty credit risk management, it has no direct bearing on PD/LGD model development, calibration, validation, or downturn estimation for corporate exposures under IRB—the core of my modelling remit.

regulatory.verdict: While ISDA SIMM is operationally relevant for our derivatives trading desk, it does not materially affect regulatory capital treatment of our Nordic corporate loan book, CRR3 implementation, or supervisory expectations from the ECB and Nordic FSAs on corporate exposures.

items.title: ISDA In Review – October 2025
items.summary: ISDA published a response to the RBI's consultation on standardized approach for counterparty credit risk (SA-CCR) in July 2025. The response broadly welcomes the RBI's move to SA-CCR and updated capital treatment for exposures under this framework. This reflects industry engagement with evolving counterparty credit risk regulation in non-EU jurisdictions.
items.why_it_matters: ISDA's position on SA-CCR implementation in India signals industry expectations on standardized approach methodology and capital treatment that may influence convergence with EU and Basel frameworks.

quant.verdict: This is principally a regulatory positioning item from an industry body on counterparty credit risk in India—SA-CCR is a standardized approach framework and the RBI consultation sits outside my IRB model scope; it has no direct bearing on our PD/LGD calibration, downturn estimation, data architecture, or supervisory model validation workflows.

regulatory.verdict: This is not relevant to my work; ISDA's engagement with RBI on Indian SA-CCR implementation does not affect the capital treatment of our Nordic corporate loan book or CRR3 timelines in the EU/EEA space.

items.title: ISDA Wins Regulation Asia’s Outstanding Contribution to Regulatory Reform Award
items.summary:
ISDA's Digital Regulatory Reporting (ISDA DRR) initiative has won an award for Outstanding Contribution to Regulatory Reform. The DRR uses machine-executable code based on the Common Domain Model to help firms comply with regulatory reporting requirements across eight jurisdictions including Australia, Hong Kong, Japan and Singapore, with plans to extend to 12 rule sets in nine jurisdictions. The initiative reduces misreporting risk and compliance costs by providing a golden-source interpretation of complex rules that firms can use for implementation or validation.</summary>
<parameter name="why_it_matters">Credit risk practitioners need awareness of this standardized reporting tool to understand emerging compliance infrastructure and potential alignment with CRR/credit risk reporting obligations as the DRR expands to additional jurisdictions and rule sets.</why_it_matters>
<parameter name="doc_status">commentary
